He provides rain on the earth, And sends water upon the fields,
So that He sets the lowly on high, And those who mourn are lifted to safety.
He thwarts the plans of the shrewd, So that their hands cannot achieve success.
He ensnares the wise in their own cunning, And the counsel of the crafty is swiftly overturned.
By day they encounter darkness, And grope at noon as in the night.
But He rescues from the sword of their mouth, And the poor from the hand of the mighty.
So the helpless has hope, And injustice shuts its mouth.
“Behold, blessed is the person whom God corrects, So do not reject the discipline of the Almighty.
For He causes pain and brings relief; He wounds, and His hands also heal.
From six troubles He will save you, Even in seven, evil will not touch you.
In famine, He will rescue you from death, And in war, from the power of the sword.
You will be hidden from the scourge of the tongue, And will not be afraid of violence when it comes.
You will laugh at destruction and hunger, And you will not fear the wild creatures.
For you will have an agreement with the stones of the field, And the animals of the field will be at peace with you.
You will know that your tent is secure, For you will visit your home and find nothing amiss.
You will also know that your descendants will be numerous, And your offspring as abundant as the grass of the earth.
You will come to the grave at a full age, Like a sheaf of grain gathered in its season.
Behold, we have examined this, and it is true; Hear it, and understand it for yourself.”
Then Job responded, saying
“If only my anguish were weighed and my calamity laid in the balances together!
For it would surely be heavier than the sand of the seas; that is why my words have been rash.
The arrows of the Almighty are within me, my spirit drinks their poison; the terrors of God are arrayed against me.
Does a wild donkey bray when it has grass, or does an ox low over its fodder?
Can tasteless food be eaten without salt, or is there any flavour in the juice of the mallow?
I refuse to touch such things; they are like loathsome food to me.
“Oh, that my request might be fulfilled, that God would grant what I long for,
that God would be willing to crush me, to let loose His hand and cut me off!
Then I would still have this consolation— my joy in relentless pain— that I have not denied the words of the Holy One.
What strength do I have, that I should hope? What is my end, that I should be patient?
Is my strength the strength of stones, or is my flesh made of bronze?
Is there any help within me, now that success has been driven away?
“For a despairing man there should be kindness from his friend, so that he does not forsake the fear of the Almighty.
But my brothers have acted deceitfully like a wadi, like the streams that overflow
when they are darkened by thawing ice and filled with melting snow,
but when they dry up, they vanish; in the heat they disappear from their place.
Their paths meander; they ascend into wasteland and are lost.
The caravans of Tema look, the travellers of Sheba hope for them.
They are put to shame because they had trusted; they arrive there, only to be disappointed.
Indeed, you have now become like them; you see something dreadful and are afraid.
Have I ever said, ‘Give me something,’ or, ‘Offer a bribe on my behalf from your wealth,’
or, ‘Deliver me from the hand of the enemy,’ or, ‘Redeem me from the clutches of the ruthless’?
“Teach me, and I will be silent; show me where I have gone wrong.
How difficult it is to hear honest words! But what do your arguments establish?
Do you intend to correct my words, and treat the words of one in despair as wind?
You would even gamble over an orphan and bargain away your friend.
But now, please look at me, and see if I am lying to your face.
Relent, let there be no injustice; reconsider, for my integrity is at stake.
Is there any wickedness on my tongue?
Does my mouth not discern calamity? “Is a human not forced to labour on earth, And are his days not like the days of a hired worker?
Just as a bond servant longs for the shade, And like a labourer who eagerly awaits his wages,
So I am given months of futility, And nights filled with trouble are assigned to me.
When I lie down, I ask, ‘When will I get up?’ But the night drags on, And I am restless until dawn.
My flesh is covered with worms and clods of dust, My skin hardens and breaks out.
My days pass more swiftly than a weaver’s shuttle, And they conclude without hope.
Remember that my life is but a breath; My eye will not see good again.
The eye of him who sees me will see me no more; Your eyes will be upon me, but I will be gone.
A cloud vanishes, and it is no more, So one who descends to Sheol does not return.
He will not come back to his house again, Nor will his place acknowledge him anymore.
“Thus, I will not hold back my words; I will speak out in the distress of my spirit, I will express my sorrow in the bitterness of my soul.
Am I the sea, or a sea creature, That You place a guard over me?
If I think, ‘My bed will comfort me, My couch will relieve my complaint,’
Then You alarm me with dreams And terrify me with visions,
So that my soul would prefer suffocation, Death rather than my suffering.
I am wasting away; I will not live forever. Leave me be, for my days are but a breath.
What is man, that You honour him, And that You care for him,
That You observe him every morning And test him at every moment?
Will You never turn Your gaze away from me, Nor leave me alone until I swallow my spit?
Have I sinned? What have I done to You, Keeper of mankind? Why have You made me Your target, So that I am a burden to myself?
Why then do You not forgive my transgression And take away my iniquity?
For now I will lie down in the dust; And You will search for me, but I will be no more.” Bildad the Shuhite responded,
“How long will you speak such things, and the words of your mouth be a strong wind?
Does God distort justice? Or does the Almighty twist what is right?
If your children sinned against Him, He delivered them to the consequences of their transgression.
But if you seek God earnestly and plead for the mercy of the Almighty,
if you are pure and upright, surely He will rouse Himself on your behalf and restore your rightful place.
Though your beginnings seemed small, your future will flourish greatly.
“Inquire of former generations, and consider what their ancestors discovered,
for we are but of yesterday and know nothing, as our days on earth are a mere shadow.
Will they not instruct you and speak to you, bringing forth words from their understanding?
Can papyrus grow tall without a marsh? Can reeds thrive without water?
While still green and uncut, they wither faster than any other plant.
Such is the fate of all who forget God; the hope of the godless shall perish.
Their confidence is fragile, and their trust is like a spider’s web.
They lean on their house, but it does not stand; they hold fast to it, but it does not endure.
They are like a plant flourishing in the sunlight, spreading its shoots over the garden.
Its roots entwine around a pile of stones, it seeks a place among the rocks.
But if it is uprooted from its place, that place will deny it, saying, ‘I never saw you.’
Surely this is the joy of its way, and from the soil others will sprout.
Behold, God will not reject a person of integrity, nor will He support evildoers.
He will once again bring laughter to your mouth and joy to your lips.
Those who oppose you will be clothed with shame, and the tents of the wicked will be no more.”
Job responded, saying,
“Truly, I know that this is so; but how can a human be justified before God?
If one desired to contend with Him, they could not answer Him once in a thousand times.
He is wise in heart and mighty in strength; who has opposed Him and remained unharmed?
He removes mountains without their knowing it, when He overturns them in His anger.
He causes the earth to tremble from its place, and its foundations quake.
He commands the sun not to shine, and seals up the stars.
He alone stretches out the heavens and walks on the waves of the sea.
He is the Creator of the Bear and Orion, the Pleiades, and the constellations of the south.
He performs great and unfathomable things, wonders without number.
If He passes by me, I would not see Him; if He goes by, I would not perceive Him.
If He takes away, who can hinder Him? Who can ask Him, ‘What are You doing?’
God does not turn back His anger; beneath Him the helpers of Rahab cower.
How then can I respond to Him, or select my words with Him?
Even if I were innocent, I could not answer Him; I would have to plead for mercy from my Judge.
If I called and He responded to me, I would not believe that He was attentive to my voice.
He would crush me with a storm and multiply my wounds without cause.
He would not allow me to catch my breath, but would fill me with bitterness.
If it is a matter of strength, He is mighty; and if it is a matter of justice, who can summon Him?
Even if I were righteous, my own mouth would condemn me; if I were guiltless, it would declare me guilty.
I am blameless, yet I do not care for myself; I despise my life.
It is all the same; therefore I say, ‘He destroys both the guiltless and the wicked.’
If a scourge brings sudden death, He mocks the despair of the innocent.
When a land is given into the hands of the wicked, He blinds its judges. If it is not He, then who is it?
“My days are swifter than a runner; they hurry away, they see no good.
They pass by like papyrus boats, like an eagle swooping down on its prey.
If I say, ‘I will put aside my complaint, I will change my expression, and smile,’
I still dread all my sufferings, for I know that You will not acquit me.
Since I am already guilty, why should I labour in vain?
Even if I washed myself with snow and cleansed my hands with lye,
You would plunge me into a pit, so that even my clothes would detest me.
For He is not a man, as I am, that I might answer Him, that we might confront each other in court.
There is no mediator between us, who might lay his hand upon us both.
Let Him remove His rod from me, and let not His dread terrify me.
Then I would speak without fear of Him, but as it stands with me, I cannot.
“In grief I loathe my life; so I will express my complaint freely; and I will speak in the bitterness of my soul.
I will ask God, ‘Do not condemn me; Help me understand why You are accusing me.
Is it right for You indeed to oppress, To reject the work of Your hands, And to look favourably on the plan of the wicked?
Do You have eyes like a man, Or do You see as mankind sees?
Are Your days those of a mortal, Or Your years like those of a man,
That You should search after my faults And probe after my every sin?
Though you know I am not guilty, And there is no one who can save me from Your hand.
‘Your hands fashioned me and made me, Yet would You now turn to destroy me?
Remember that You made me as clay; And would You turn me into dust again?
Did You not form me like milk, And solidify me like cheese;
Cover me with skin and flesh, And weave me together with bones and sinews?
You have granted me life and kindness; And Your care has preserved my spirit.
Yet these things You have hidden in Your heart; I know that this is within You:
If I sin, then You would take note of me, And would not absolve me of my guilt.
If I am wicked, woe to me! But if I am righteous, I dare not lift up my head. I am full of shame, and aware of my misery.
Should my head rise high, You would hunt me like a lion, And again show Your power against me.
You renew Your witnesses against me And increase Your anger toward me; Hardship after hardship comes upon me.
Why then have You brought me out of the womb? If only I had died, and no eye had seen me!
I should have been as though I had not been, Brought from the womb to the grave.’
Are my days not few? Stop, leave me alone, so that I may have a little cheer
Before I go—and I shall not return— To the realm of darkness and deep shadow,
The land of utter gloom, like darkness itself, Of deep shadow without order, Where even light is like darkness.”
Zophar the Naamathite responded:
“Should a torrent of words remain unanswered, and should a man full of talk be justified?
Should your empty chatter silence others? Will no one correct you when you mock?
You claim, ‘My doctrine is pure, and I am clean in your sight.’
Oh, that God would speak and open His lips against you,
and reveal to you the secrets of wisdom, for true wisdom has two sides. Be assured that God has overlooked some of your iniquity.
“Can you comprehend the mysteries of God? Can you explore the limits of the Almighty?
They are higher than the heavens—what can you achieve? They are deeper than the grave—what can you understand?
Their scope is longer than the earth and broader than the sea.
If He passes by and imprisons or calls a court, who can oppose Him?
For He knows deceitful men; He sees wickedness without needing to examine it.
A senseless person will gain understanding when a wild donkey’s colt is born a man.
“If you prepare your heart and stretch out your hands towards Him,
if there is any wrong in your hand, remove it, and do not let wickedness reside in your tents,
then you will lift up your face without shame; you will stand firm and without fear.
You will forget your misery, remembering it like waters that have passed away.
Your life will be brighter than the noonday; even darkness will be like the morning.
You will feel secure, for there is hope; you will look around and rest in safety.
You will lie down, and none will make you afraid, and many will seek your favour.
But the eyes of the wicked will fail, and escape will elude them; their hope will be to breathe their last.”
Then Job responded, saying,
“Surely, you are the people, and wisdom will perish with you!
But I have understanding as well as you; I am not beneath you. Who does not know such things as these?
I have become a joke to my friends, I who called upon God and was answered— a just and blameless man has become a joke.
Those who are at ease hold calamity in contempt, as it is prepared for those whose feet are slipping.
The tents of robbers are at peace, and those who provoke God are secure, whom God has brought into their power.
“But ask the animals, and they will teach you; or the birds in the sky, and they will inform you.
Or speak to the earth, and it will teach you; and let the fish of the sea declare to you.
Who among all these does not accept that the hand of the Lord is responsible for this?
In His hand is the life of every creature and the breath of all humanity.
Does the ear not test words as the palate tastes food?
Wisdom is found with the aged, and understanding comes with long life.
“To Him belong wisdom and might; counsel and understanding are His.
What He breaks down cannot be rebuilt; the person He imprisons cannot be released.
If He restrains the waters, they dry up; if He releases them, they overwhelm the earth.
To Him belong strength and sound wisdom; both the deceived and the deceiver are His.
He leads counsellors away barefoot and makes judges into fools.
He removes the shackles of kings and binds a loincloth around their waist.
He leads priests away barefoot and overthrows those long established.
He silences the speech of trusted advisers and removes the discernment of elders.
He pours contempt upon nobles and loosens the belt of the mighty.
He reveals deep things from the darkness and brings the hidden darkness into light.
He makes nations great, then destroys them; He enlarges nations, then disperses them.
He deprives the leaders of the earth of their reason and makes them wander in a trackless wasteland.
They grope in darkness without light; He makes them stagger like drunkards.
“See, my eye has witnessed all this, My ear has listened and comprehended it.
What you know, I also understand; I am not beneath you.
Yet I wish to speak to the Almighty, And I long to reason with God.
But you cover me with falsehoods; You are all ineffective healers.
If only you would remain silent, That would indeed be your wisdom!
Kindly hear my plea, And attend to the arguments of my lips.
Will you speak unjustly on God's behalf, And speak deceitfully for Him?
Will you show favouritism for Him? Will you argue for God?
Will it be well when He examines you? Or will you mislead Him as one misleads a man?
He will surely correct you If you secretly show favouritism.
Will His majesty not frighten you, And the fear of Him fall upon you?
Your memorable sayings are proverbs of ashes; Your defences are defences of clay.
“Be silent before me so that I may speak; Then let whatever may come upon me.
Why should I risk my life, And place my life in my own hands?
Though He may slay me, I will trust in Him. Nevertheless, I will present my case before Him.
This too will be my deliverance, For a godless person cannot stand before Him.
Pay close attention to my words, And let my declaration reach your ears.
See now, I have prepared my case; I am confident that I will be justified.
Who will argue against me? For then I would be silent and perish.
“Only two things do not do to me, Then I will not hide from Your presence:
Withdraw Your hand from me, And do not let Your fear overwhelm me.
Then call, and I will respond; Or let me speak, then answer me.
How numerous are my transgressions and sins? Reveal to me my offences and my sin.
Why do You conceal Your face And regard me as Your adversary?
Will You frighten a windblown leaf? Or will You chase after dry chaff?
For You inscribe bitter things against me And make me bear the consequences of my youth.
You confine my feet in shackles And observe all my paths; You set a boundary for the soles of my feet,
While I am wasting away like something decayed, Like a garment that is moth-eaten.
“Human beings, born of women, Have brief lives and are filled with trouble.
Like a flower, they emerge and wither, They also flee like a shadow and do not endure.
You also observe them closely And bring them into judgment with Yourself.
Who can bring purity from impurity? No one can!
Since their days are determined, The number of their months is known to You; And You have set their boundaries which they cannot exceed.
Turn Your gaze from them so they may have rest, Until they complete their day like a hired labourer.
“For there is hope for a tree, If it is cut down, that it will sprout again, And its shoots will not fail.
Though its root grows old in the ground, And its stump dies in the dry soil,
At the scent of water, it will thrive And send out branches like a young plant.
But when a person dies and lies low, A person passes away, and where are they?
As water vanishes from the sea, And a river becomes dry and parched,
So people lie down and do not rise. Until the heavens are no more, They will not awaken nor be roused from their slumber.
“Oh that You would hide me in Sheol, That You would conceal me until Your anger subsides, That You would set a time for me and remember me!
If a person dies, will they live again? All the days of my hardship I will wait Until my renewal comes.
You will call, and I will answer You; You will yearn for the work of Your hands.
For now You count my steps, You do not watch over my sin.
My transgression is sealed up in a bag, And You cover over my iniquity.
“But a mountain collapses and crumbles away, And a rock is moved from its place;
Water erodes stones, Its torrents wash away the soil of the earth; So You diminish a person's hope.
You continually overpower them and they depart; You alter their appearance and send them away.
Their children attain honour, but they do not know it; Or they become insignificant, and they do not perceive it.
However, their body feels pain, And their soul mourns over it.”
Then Eliphaz the Temanite spoke:
“Should a wise person respond with empty ideas, and fill themselves with the hot east wind?
Should they engage in fruitless talk, with words that bring no benefit?
You indeed diminish reverence and obstruct meditation before God.
Your wrongdoing instructs your speech, and you choose the tongue of the crafty.
Your own mouth condemns you, not I; your own lips bear witness against you.
“Were you the first human ever born? Were you brought forth before the hills?
Do you listen in on God’s secret council? Do you claim exclusive wisdom?
What do you know that we do not? What insights do you possess that we lack?
The grey- haired and the aged are among us, older than your father.
Are the consolations of God too small for you, or the gentle word spoken to you?
Why does your heart lead you astray, and why do your eyes flash,
that you turn your spirit against God and utter such words?
What is humanity, that they could be pure, or those born of a woman, that they could be righteous?
If He places no trust in His holy ones, and even the heavens are not pure in His sight,
how much less mortals, who are vile and corrupt, who drink iniquity like water!
“Listen to me and I will explain; I will declare what I have observed,
what the wise have proclaimed, and have not concealed from their ancestors,
to whom alone the land was given when no stranger passed among them:
The wicked person suffers torment all their days, and the years reserved for the ruthless are limited.
Terrifying sounds fill his ears; when he is at peace, marauders attack him.
He does not anticipate freedom from darkness; he is marked for the sword.
He wanders for food, saying, ‘Where is it?’ He knows the day of darkness is near.
Distress and anguish terrify him; they overpower him like a king ready for battle,
because he has stretched out his hand against God, and shown arrogance towards the Almighty.
He charges defiantly at Him with a thick, strong shield.
Even though his face is covered with fat and his waist is full of flesh,
he will dwell in desolate cities, in houses no one inhabits, destined to become ruins.
He will not remain wealthy, nor will his riches endure; his possessions will not spread across the land.
He will not find refuge from the darkness; a flame will wither his shoots, and the breath of God’s mouth will carry him away.
Let him not deceive himself by trusting in vanity, for he will receive nothing in return.
It will be fulfilled before his time, and his branches will not flourish.
He will be like a vine stripped of its immature grapes, like an olive tree shedding its blossoms.
For the company of the godless is barren, and fire consumes the tents of those who love bribes.
They conceive trouble and give birth to wrongdoing; their mind prepares deceit.”
Job responded, saying,
“I have listened to much of this; you are all ineffective comforters!
Is there no end to your long-winded speeches? What provokes you to keep arguing?
I too could speak as you do, if you were in my place. I could make eloquent speeches against you and shake my head at you.
But I would encourage you with my mouth, and the comfort of my lips would bring you relief.
“If I speak, my suffering is not eased, and if I remain silent, it does not leave me.
Surely, now God has worn me out; you have devastated my entire household.
You have shrivelled me up, and it has become a witness; my gauntness rises up and testifies against me.
In his anger, God has torn me and hunted me down; he gnashes his teeth at me. My adversary glares at me.
People gape at me with their mouths; they strike my cheek in scorn and gather themselves against me.
God has handed me over to the wicked and thrown me into the hands of the ungodly.
I was at ease, but he shattered me; he seized me by the neck and crushed me. He has made me his target.
His archers surround me. Without mercy, he pierces my kidneys and spills my bile on the ground.
He breaks through me breach upon breach; he rushes at me like a warrior.
I have sewn sackcloth over my skin and buried my brow in the dust.
My face is flushed with weeping, and deep shadows encircle my eyes,
although my hands are free of violence and my prayer is pure.
“O earth, do not cover my blood; may my cry find no resting place!
Yet, my witness is in the heavens; my advocate resides on high.
My friends are my scoffers; my eyes pour out tears to God,
that one might plead for a man with God as one pleads for a friend.
For after a few years have passed, I shall go the way of no return.
“My spirit is crushed, my days are extinguished, The grave awaits me.
Certainly mockers are with me, And my eye gazes on their provocation.
Please lay down a pledge for me with Yourself; Who is there that will be my guarantor?
For You have kept their heart away from understanding, Therefore You will not exalt them.
He who informs against friends for a share of the spoil, The eyes of his children will perish.
Yet He has made me a byword among the people, And I am one at whom people spit.
My eye has also grown dim because of grief, And all my body parts are like a shadow.
The upright will be astonished at this, And the innocent will rise up against the godless.
Nevertheless the righteous will remain steadfast in his way, And the one who has clean hands will become ever stronger.
Yet return, all of you, I pray, For I do not find a wise man among you.
My days are past, my plans are shattered, The desires of my heart.
They turn night into day, saying, ‘The light is near,’ in the presence of darkness.
If I look to Sheol as my home, I spread my bed in the darkness;
I call to the grave, ‘You are my father’; To the maggot, ‘my mother and my sister.’
Where then is my hope? And who see any hope for me?
Will it descend with me to Sheol?
Shall we together go down into the dust?” Then Bildad the Shuhite replied and said:
“How long will you keep searching for words? Be prudent, and then we can converse.
Why are we viewed as beasts, and considered foolish in your eyes?
You who tear yourself in anger, should the earth be forsaken for your sake, or the rock be displaced from its position?
“The light of the wicked is extinguished, and the flame of his fire ceases to shine.
The light in his tent is dimmed, and the lamp above him is put out.
His vigorous stride is curtailed, and his own plans bring him down.
His feet lead him into a net, and he walks into its mesh.
A snare catches him by the heel, and a trap holds him tightly.
A noose is hidden for him on the ground, and a trap lies on his path.
Sudden terrors frighten him on every side and pursue him at every step.
His strength is depleted, and calamity is ready at his side.
It consumes parts of his skin; death’s firstborn devours his limbs.
He is torn from the security of his tent and led before the king of terrors.
Brimstone is scattered on his dwelling; fire consumes his tent.
His roots wither below, and his branches fade above.
His memory fades from the earth, and he leaves no name in the land.
He is cast from light into darkness and removed from the world.
He has no offspring or descendants among his people, nor any survivor where he lived.
Those in the west are dismayed at his fate, and those in the east are filled with dread.
Surely these are the dwellings of the wicked, and this is the place of one who does not know God.”
Then Job responded, saying,
“How long will you torment me and crush me with your words?
You have reproached me these ten times; you are not ashamed to mistreat me.
Even if I have erred, my mistake concerns me alone.
If indeed you exalt yourselves over me and use my disgrace against me,
then understand that I feel wronged by God and surrounded by His net.
“Though I cry out, ‘Violence!’ I receive no answer; I call for help, but there is no justice.
He has blocked my path so I cannot pass, and He has shrouded my ways in darkness.
He has stripped me of my honour and taken the crown from my head.
He tears me down on all sides, and I am gone; He uproots my hope like a tree.
His anger burns against me; He regards me as His enemy.
His troops advance together; they construct a siege ramp against me and encamp around my tent.
“He has alienated my brothers from me, and my acquaintances are estranged from me.
Those who are related to me have let me down; my close friends have forgotten me.
The dwellers in my house and my maidservants consider me a stranger; I am a foreigner in their sight.
I call to my servant, but he gives no answer; I must plead with him with my own mouth.
My breath is offensive to my wife, and I am loathsome to my own family.
Even young children despise me; when I stand up, they speak against me.
All my close friends abhor me; those I love have turned against me.
I am reduced to skin and bones; I have escaped with only the skin of my teeth.
“Show me compassion, my friends, show compassion, for the hand of God has struck me.
Why do you pursue me as God does? Are you not satisfied with my flesh?
“Oh, that my words were recorded, that they were inscribed in a book,
that they were engraved with an iron tool and lead, or carved in the rock forever!
For I know that my Redeemer lives, and that in the end He will stand upon the earth.
And after my skin has been destroyed, yet in my flesh I shall see God;
I myself will see Him with my own eyes — I, and not another. My heart yearns within me!
If you say, ‘How shall we persecute him, since the root of the trouble is found in him,’
then be cautious of the sword for yourselves, for wrath brings the consequences of the sword, so that you may understand there is judgment.”
Zophar the Naamathite responded,
“My restless thoughts compel me to respond, because of my deep unease.
I have heard a rebuke that insults me, and the insight of my understanding urges me to reply.
Do you not know this from ancient times, since mankind was placed upon the earth,
that the exultation of the wicked is fleeting, and the joy of the godless is but for a moment?
Though his conceit reaches the heavens and his head touches the clouds,
he will vanish forever like his own waste; those who have seen him will say, ‘Where is he?’
He will vanish like a dream, and cannot be found; like a vision of the night, he is chased away.
The eye that saw him will see him no more; his place will behold him no longer.
His children will seek the favour of the poor, and his hands will return his wealth.
His bones, once full of youthful vigour, will lie down with him in the dust.
“Though wrongdoing is sweet in his mouth and he hides it under his tongue,
though he cherishes it and does not let it go, but keeps it in his mouth,
yet his food will turn sour in his stomach; it will become the venom of cobras within him.
He swallows riches, but will vomit them up; God will force them out of his belly.
He will draw in the venom of cobras; the viper's tongue will bring him down.
He will not gaze upon the streams, the rivers flowing with honey and cream.
He will return what he laboured for and will not consume it; he will not enjoy the wealth from his trading.
For he has crushed and abandoned the poor; he has seized a house he did not build.
“Because he knew no peace within himself, he will not retain anything he desires.
Nothing remains for him to devour; thus his prosperity will not endure.
In the midst of his abundance, distress will overtake him; the full force of misery will come upon him.
When he has filled his belly, God will unleash His fierce anger upon him and rain it down while he is eating.
Though he flees from an iron weapon, a bronze-tipped arrow will strike him.
It is drawn and comes out of his back, the flashing point from his liver; terrors will come upon him.
Complete darkness is reserved for his treasures; an unfanned fire will consume him and devour what remains in his tent.
The heavens will expose his wrongdoing, and the earth will rise up against him.
The increase of his house will flow away; his possessions will vanish on the day of God's wrath.
This is the portion allotted by God to the wicked, the inheritance decreed to him by God.”
Job responded:
“Listen intently to my words, and let this be your way of comforting me.
Allow me to speak, and after I have spoken, you may mock me.
Is my complaint directed to a mere human? Why should I not be impatient?
Look at me and be astonished; place your hand over your mouth.
When I think of it, I am disturbed; horror grips my flesh.
Why do the wicked continue to live, grow old, and increase in power?
Their children thrive around them, their offspring are before their eyes.
Their homes are secure and free from fear; the rod of God is not upon them.
Their bulls breed successfully; their cows calve and do not lose their young.
They send forth their children like a flock; their little ones dance.
They sing to the tambourine and lyre, and rejoice to the sound of the flute.
They spend their days in prosperity and descend peacefully to the grave.
Yet they say to God, ‘Depart from us! We have no desire to know your ways.
Who is the Almighty, that we should serve him? What profit do we have if we pray to him?’
Indeed, their prosperity is not in their own hands, so I distance myself from the counsel of the wicked.
“How often is the lamp of the wicked extinguished? How often does calamity befall them, apportioned by God in his anger?
Are they as straw before the wind, like chaff swept away by a storm?
It is said, ‘God stores up a person's iniquity for their children.’ Let him repay them so that they may know it themselves.
Let their own eyes see their downfall; let them drink of the wrath of the Almighty.
For what do they care about their household after them, when their allotted months have ended?
Can anyone impart knowledge to God, since he judges even the highest?
One person dies in full strength, completely at ease and secure,
his body well nourished, bones rich with marrow.
Another dies with a troubled soul, never having enjoyed anything good.
Together they lie in the dust, and worms cover them both.
“Behold, I know your thoughts and the schemes you devise against me.
You ask, ‘Where now is the house of the noble, the tents where the wicked lived?’
Have you not questioned those who travel? Have you not considered their accounts—
that the wicked are spared from the day of calamity, and are led away from the day of wrath?
Who confronts them with their actions, and who repays them for what they have done?
They are carried to the grave, and a watch is kept over their tombs.
The earth in the valley is sweet to them; all humanity follows after them, and countless others precede them.
So how can you offer me empty comfort?
Your answers are nothing but falsehood!” Now Eliphaz the Temanite spoke,
“Can a man be of any use to God? Can a wise person be beneficial to himself?
Does it bring any pleasure to the Almighty if you are righteous, or any gain if your ways are perfect?
Is it because of your reverence that He corrects you and enters into judgement with you?
Is not your wickedness vast, and are not your iniquities without end?
For you have taken pledges from your kin without cause and stripped the naked of their clothing.
You have failed to provide water for the weary, and you have denied bread to the hungry.
But the land belongs to the powerful, and the honourable dwell on it.
You have sent widows away empty-handed, and the strength of the fatherless has been crushed.
That is why snares are around you, and sudden terror overwhelms you,
or darkness so that you cannot see, and a flood of waters covers you.
“Is not God in the heights of heaven? And behold the highest stars, how lofty they are!
Yet you question, ‘What does God know? Can He judge through the dense darkness?
Clouds are a covering for Him, so that He does not see us, and He walks on the vault of heaven.’
Will you continue on the ancient path that the wicked have trodden,
who were carried away before their time, whose foundations were swept away by a flood?
They told God, ‘Leave us alone!’ and ‘What can the Almighty do to them?’
Yet it was He who filled their houses with good things, but the counsel of the wicked is far from me.
The righteous see it and are glad, and the innocent mock them, saying,
‘Surely our adversaries are cut off, and fire consumes their wealth.’
“Acquaint yourself with Him and be at peace; thereby good will come to you.
Accept guidance from His mouth and treasure His words in your heart.
If you return to the Almighty, you will be restored; if you remove iniquity far from your tent,
and cast your gold to the dust, the gold of Ophir to the stones of the brooks,
then the Almighty will be your treasure, and precious silver to you.
For then you will find joy in the Almighty and lift up your face to God.
You will call upon Him, and He will answer you, and you will fulfil your vows.
You will decree a thing, and it will be established for you, and light will shine on your ways.
When people are humbled, you will say, ‘Lift them up!’ and He will save the lowly.
He will deliver even the guilty, who will be saved by the cleanness of your hands.”
Job responded,
“Today also my complaint is bitter; His hand is heavy despite my sighing.
If only I knew how to find Him, that I might reach His dwelling!
I would present my case to Him and speak with conviction.
I would know the words He would reply to me, and consider what He would say.
Would He oppose me with great power? No, He would surely give heed to me.
There the upright could engage with Him, and I would be set free forever from my judge.
“Behold, I go east but He is not there, and west, but I cannot find Him;
When He works in the north, I do not see Him; He turns to the south, but I do not behold Him.
Yet He knows the path I take; when He has tested me, I shall emerge as gold.
My feet have held fast to His steps; I have kept His way and not turned aside.
I have not departed from the command of His lips; I have treasured the words of His mouth more than my daily sustenance.
But He is unchangeable, and who can turn Him? He does whatever He desires.
He will complete what He has planned for me, and many such things are with Him.
Therefore, I am terrified at His presence; when I consider this, I am in dread of Him.
It is God who has made my heart faint, and the Almighty who has frightened me,
Yet I am not silenced by the darkness, nor by the deep gloom that covers my face.
“Why are times not appointed by the Almighty, And why do those who know Him not perceive His days?
People move boundary stones; They seize and consume flocks.
They drive away the donkeys of the orphans, They seize the widow’s ox as a pledge.
They push the needy off the road; The poor of the land are made to hide together.
Behold, like wild donkeys in the wilderness They search for food in their activity, As sustenance for their children in the desert.
They gather their fodder in the field And glean the vineyard of the unjust.
They spend the night without clothing, And have no covering against the cold.
They are drenched by the mountain rains, And they cling to the rock for lack of a shelter.
Others snatch an orphan from the breast, And seize it as a pledge against the poor.
They cause the poor to go about without clothing, And they take away the sheaves from the hungry.
Within the walls they press oil; They tread wine presses but remain thirsty.
From the city people groan, And the souls of the wounded cry out; Yet God does not regard the wrongdoing.
Others have been with those who rebel against the light; They do not want to know its ways, And do not stay in its paths.
The murderer rises at dawn; He kills the poor and the needy, And at night he behaves like a thief.
The eye of the adulterer waits for twilight, Saying, ‘No eye will see me.’ And he disguises his face.
In the dark they break into houses, They shut themselves up by day; They do not know the light.
For the morning is the same to him as deep darkness, For he is familiar with the terrors of deep darkness.
They are insignificant on the surface of the water; Their plot of land is cursed on the earth. They do not turn toward the vineyards.
Dryness and heat consume the snow waters, So Sheol consumes those who have sinned.
A mother will forget him; the worm feeds sweetly on him. He is no longer remembered. And wickedness is broken like a tree.
He mistreats the childless woman, And does no good for the widow.
But He drags off the strong by His power; He rises, but no one has assurance of life.
He grants them security, and they are supported; And His eyes are on their ways.
They are exalted a little while, then they are gone; Moreover, they are brought low, like everything gathered up; Even like the heads of grain they are cut off.
Now if it is not so, who can prove me false, And make my words meaningless?”
Bildad the Shuhite responded, saying,
“Dominion and awe belong to Him who brings order to the heights.
Is there a limit to His armies? Upon whom does His light not shine?
How then can a human be just before God? How can one born of a woman be clean?
If even the moon lacks brightness and the stars are not pure in His view,
how much less a human, who is a maggot, and a son of man, who is a worm!”
Job responded,
“What assistance you have given to the weak! How you have strengthened the arm that lacks power!
What counsel you have provided to the one without wisdom! What abundant insight you have shown!
Whom did you address with these words? And whose spirit has spoken through you?
“The spirits of the departed tremble beneath the waters and their inhabitants.
The realm of Sheol is exposed before Him; Abaddon is laid bare.
He stretches out the northern skies over the void and suspends the earth upon nothing.
He binds up the waters in His clouds, yet the clouds do not burst under their weight.
He conceals the face of the full moon, spreading His cloud over it.
He has drawn a circle on the surface of the waters, marking the boundary between light and darkness.
The pillars of the heavens shake, astounded at His rebuke.
By His power, He stirs the sea; by His wisdom, He shattered Rahab.
By His breath, the skies are made clear; His hand pierced the swift serpent.
Indeed, these are but the edges of His ways; and how faint is the whisper we hear of Him!
But the thunder of His power, who can comprehend?” Then Job again took up his discourse and said,
“As surely as God lives, who has denied me justice, And the Almighty, who has filled my soul with bitterness,
For as long as I have life within me, And the breath of the Almighty in my nostrils,
My lips will not speak falsehood, Nor will my tongue utter deceit.
It is far from me to say you are right; Until I die, I will not abandon my integrity.
I hold fast to my righteousness and will not let it slip. My heart does not reproach me for any of my days.
Let my enemy be like the wicked, And my adversary like the unjust.
For what hope has the godless when his life comes to an end, When God demands his life?
Will God listen to his cry When trouble befalls him?
Will he find joy in the Almighty? Will he call upon God at all times?
I will teach you about the power of God; I will not hide what is with the Almighty.
Behold, all of you have now seen it; Why then do you speak of empty nonsense?
This is the fate of a wicked person from God, And the inheritance which oppressors receive from the Almighty:
Though his children are many, they are destined for the sword; And his offspring will not have enough bread.
His survivors will be buried by the plague, And their widows will not be able to mourn.
Though he heaps up silver like dust, And amasses fabrics as plentiful as clay,
He may gather it, but the righteous will wear it, And the innocent will share the silver.
He builds his house like a spider’s web, Or a shelter made by a watchman.
He lies down wealthy, but never again; He opens his eyes, and it is gone.
Terrors overtake him like a flood; A storm sweeps him away in the night.
The east wind carries him off, and he vanishes; It sweeps him out of his place.
It hurls itself against him without pity; He will surely try to escape its power.
People will clap their hands at him, And will hiss at him from their places.
“Indeed, there is a mine for silver And a location for purifying gold.
Iron is extracted from the earth, And copper is refined from stone.
Man brings an end to darkness, And to the farthest limit he searches out The rock in gloom and deep shadow.
He sinks a shaft far from habitation, Forgotten by the foot; They hang and swing to and fro far from people.
The earth, from it comes food, And underneath it is turned over as fire.
Its rocks yield sapphires, And its dust holds gold.
The path is unknown to birds of prey, Nor has the falcon’s eye seen it.
The proud animals have not walked on it, Nor has the lion passed over it.
He lays his hand on the flint; He overturns the mountains at their base.
He cuts out channels through the rocks, And his eye discovers all that is precious.
He stops the streams from flowing, And brings to light what was hidden.
“But where is wisdom to be found? And where is the place of understanding?
Humanity does not know its worth, Nor is it located in the land of the living.
The depths of the ocean declare, ‘It is not within me’; And the sea proclaims, ‘It is not with me.’
Pure gold cannot be exchanged for it, Nor can silver be weighed as its value.
It cannot be esteemed in the gold of Ophir, In precious onyx, or sapphire.
Gold or glass cannot compare to it, Nor can it be traded for items of fine gold.
Coral and crystal are not to be mentioned; And the gaining of wisdom is more precious than pearls.
The topaz of Cush cannot compare to it, Nor can it be esteemed in pure gold.
“Then from where does wisdom arise? And where is the place of understanding?
It remains hidden from the eyes of every living being, And is concealed from the birds of the sky.
Abaddon and Death say, ‘With our ears we have heard a rumour of it.’
God comprehends its path, And He knows its place.
For He observes to the ends of the earth; He sees everything under the heavens.
When He gave the wind its weight, And measured the waters by measure,
When He set a boundary for the rain, And a path for the thunderbolt,
Then He saw it and declared it; He established it and also examined it.
And to all of mankind He said, ‘Behold, the fear of the Lord, that is wisdom; And to turn away from evil is understanding.’”
Then Job again took up his discourse and said,
“If only I could be as in the months of old, As in the days when God watched over me;
When His lamp shone above me, And by His light I walked through darkness;
Just as I was in the days of my youth, When God's protection was over my tent;
When the Almighty was still with me, And my children surrounded me;
When my steps were bathed in butter, And the rock poured out streams of oil for me!
When I went to the city gate, When I took my seat in the public square,
The young men saw me and withdrew, And the old men arose and stood.
The leaders ceased speaking And placed their hands on their mouths;
The voices of the nobles were hushed, And their tongues clung to their palates.
For when an ear heard, it called me blessed, And when an eye saw, it gave witness of me,
Because I delivered the poor who cried for help, And the orphan who had no supporter.
The blessing of the one who was perishing came upon me, And I made the widow’s heart sing for joy.
I wore righteousness, and it clothed me; My justice was like a robe and a headband.
I was a guide to the blind, And a support to the lame.
I was a father to the needy, And I examined the case I did not know.
I shattered the jaws of the wicked, And rescued the prey from their teeth.
Then I thought, ‘I will die in my nest, And I will multiply my days like the sand.
My roots spread out to the waters, And dew lies on my branches all night.
My glory is ever fresh with me, And my bow is renewed in my hand.’
To me they listened and waited, And remained silent for my advice.
After my words they did not speak again, And my speech settled on them.
They waited for me as for the rain, And opened their mouths as for the spring rain.
I smiled at them when they did not believe, And they did not look at the light of my face unkindly.
I determined their course and sat as chief, And dwelt as a king among the troops, As one who comforted the mourners.
“But now those younger than I ridicule me, Whose fathers I would not have placed with the dogs of my flock.
Indeed, what benefit was the strength of their hands to me? Their vigour had departed from them.
From poverty and hunger they are gaunt— They who gnaw the dry ground by night in devastation and desolation,
Who gather saltweed by the bushes, And whose food is the root of the broom shrub.
They are driven from the community; People shout against them as against a thief,
So that they dwell in desolate valleys, In holes of the earth and of the rocks.
Among the bushes they wail; Under the weeds they huddle together.
Sons of the foolish, even sons of the nameless, They have been driven out of the land.
And now I have become their ridicule, And I have become a byword to them.
They detest me and keep their distance from me, And they do not refrain from spitting in my face.
Because He has loosened His bowstring and afflicted me, They have cast off restraint in my presence.
On the right hand their brood arises; They push aside my feet and build up against me their ways of destruction.
They disrupt my path, They gain from my downfall; No one holds them back.
They come upon me like a breach in the wall; They advance in the storm.
Sudden terrors are turned against me; They pursue my honour like the wind, And my prosperity has passed away like a cloud.
And now my soul is poured out within me; Days of suffering have overtaken me.
At night it pierces my bones within me, And my relentless pains do not rest.
By a great force my garment is distorted; It binds me like the collar of my coat.
He has thrown me into the mire, And I am reduced to dust and ashes.
I cry out to You for help, yet You do not respond; I stand up, and You turn Your gaze against me.
You have become harsh to me; With the strength of Your hand You persecute me.
You lift me up to the wind and make me ride on it, And You scatter me in the storm.
For I know that You will bring me to death, And to the house of meeting for all the living.
Yet does one not stretch out his hand in a heap of ruins, Or in his disaster therefore cry out for help?
Have I not wept for the one in distress? Was my soul not grieved for the needy?
When I hoped for good, evil came; When I waited for light, darkness came.
My internal organs are in turmoil and are never still; Days of suffering confront me.
I wander about in mourning without solace; I stand up in the assembly and plead for help.
I have become akin to jackals, And a companion to ostriches.
My skin has turned dark upon me, And my bones are burning with fever.
Thus my harp is tuned to mourning, And my flute to the sound of lamentation.
“I have made an agreement with my eyes; How then could I gaze upon a young woman?
And what is the portion given by God from above, Or the inheritance from the Almighty on high?
Is it not disaster for the unjust, And misfortune for those who work iniquity?
Does He not observe my ways, And count all my steps?
If I have walked in falsehood, And my foot has hastened after deceit,
Let Him weigh me with honest scales, And let God know my integrity.
If my step has strayed from the path, Or my heart followed my eyes, Or any stain has clung to my hands,
Let me sow and another consume, And let my produce be uprooted.
If my heart has been lured by a woman, Or I have waited at my neighbour’s doorway,
May my wife grind for another, And let others bow down over her.
For that would be a shameful act; Moreover, it would be wrongdoing punishable by judges.
For it would be a fire that consumes to destruction, And would uproot all my increase.
If I have disregarded the plea of my male or female servants When they brought a complaint against me,
What then could I do when God rises up? And when He calls me to account, how shall I answer Him?
Did He who made me in the womb not make them, And did not the same one form us in the womb?
If I have denied the poor their desires, Or caused the widow to lose hope,
Or have eaten my morsel alone, And not shared it with the orphan
(But from my youth he grew up with me as with a father, And from my mother’s womb I guided her),
If I have seen anyone perish for lack of clothing, Or a needy person without a cloak,
If his waist has not blessed me, Or he has not been warmed with the fleece of my sheep,
If I have raised my hand against the orphan, Because I saw I had support at the gate,
May my shoulder dislocate, And my arm be broken off at the elbow.
For the fear of calamity from God is overwhelming to me, And because of His majesty, I am powerless.
If I have placed my trust in gold, And called fine gold my security,
If I have rejoiced because my wealth was great, And because my hand had gained so much,
If I have been captivated by the sun when it shone Or the moon in its splendour,
And my heart was secretly drawn, And my hand offered a kiss from my mouth,
That too would have been a sin deserving judgment, For I would have denied God above.
Have I rejoiced at the downfall of my enemy, Or become glad when trouble found him?
No, I have not permitted my mouth to sin By asking for his life in a curse.
Have the people of my tent not said, ‘Who can find one who has not been satisfied with his food’?
The stranger has not spent the night outside, For I have opened my doors to the traveller.
Have I concealed my transgressions like Adam, By hiding my guilt in my heart,
Because I was afraid of the large crowd, And the scorn of families unnerved me, And I kept silent and did not go out of doors?
Oh that I had someone to hear me! Here is my mark; Let the Almighty respond to me! And the indictment which my adversary has written,
I would certainly carry it on my shoulder, I would bind it to myself like a crown.
I would recount to Him all my steps; Like a prince, I would approach Him.
If my land cries out against me, And its furrows join in lament,
If I have consumed its produce without payment, Or have caused its owners to lose their lives,
May thorns grow instead of wheat, And weeds instead of barley.”
The words of Job are ended. Thus, the three men ceased responding to Job, for he was righteous in his own eyes.
However, Elihu son of Barakel the Buzite, from the family of Ram, was incensed with Job because he justified himself rather than God.
His anger also kindled against the three friends, as they had found no answer to refute Job, yet they had condemned him.
Elihu had held back from speaking to Job because they were older than he.
But when Elihu observed that the three men had no further responses, his anger was ignited.
So Elihu son of Barakel the Buzite declared: “I am young in years, and you are aged; hence, I was timid and refrained from expressing my thoughts.
I thought, ‘Let age speak, and let many years impart wisdom.’
But it is the spirit in a person, the breath of the Almighty, that bestows understanding.
It is not just the elderly who are wise, nor is it only the aged who comprehend justice.
Therefore, I say: ‘Hear me out; I too will share what I know.’
I waited for your words, I listened to your considered speech, while you searched for what to say.
I gave you my full attention. Yet not one of you has proven Job wrong; none has answered his arguments.
Therefore, do not claim, ‘We have discovered wisdom; let God, not man, defeat him.’
But Job has not directed his words against me, and I will not respond to him with your arguments.
They are dismayed and have ceased to answer; words have left them.
Should I wait, now that they are silent, now that they stand without a reply?
I too will have my say; I also will share my thoughts.
For I am filled with words, and the spirit within me urges me to speak.
Inside, I am like wine that has no vent, like new wineskins ready to burst.
I must speak to find relief; I must open my lips and respond.
I will show no partiality, nor will I flatter any man.
For I do not know how to flatter; if I did, my Maker would swiftly take me away.
“But now, Job, please listen to my speech; pay attention to all my words.
See now, I am about to open my mouth; my tongue is ready to speak.
My words come from the integrity of my heart; my lips express knowledge sincerely.
The Spirit of God has created me; the breath of the Almighty gives me life.
Respond to me if you can; prepare yourself and take your stand.
I am just like you before God; I too have been formed from clay.
No fear of me should alarm you, nor should my hand be heavy upon you.
“But you have spoken in my hearing, and I have heard the very words you said:
‘I am pure, without transgression; I am clean and there is no iniquity in me.
Yet He finds occasions against me; He regards me as His adversary.
He puts my feet in shackles; He watches all my paths.’
“But I say to you, in this you are not right, for God is greater than any human.
Why do you contend with Him, saying He does not answer any of your words?
For God does speak—now one way, now another—yet no one notices it.
In a dream, a vision of the night, when deep sleep falls upon people, while they slumber on their beds,
He may open their ears and terrify them with warnings,
to turn a person from wrongdoing and to keep them from pride,
to preserve their soul from the pit, and their life from perishing by the sword.
“Or they may be chastened on a bed of pain, with constant distress in their bones,
so that their being loathes bread, and their soul despises their favourite food.
Their flesh wastes away to nothing, and their bones, once unseen, now stick out.
Their soul draws near to the pit, and their life to the messengers of death.
Yet if there is an angel on their side, a mediator, one out of a thousand, to show them what is right,
and he is gracious to them, and says, ‘Deliver them from going down to the pit; I have found a ransom for them—
let their flesh be renewed like a child’s; let them return to the days of their youthful vigour’—
then they will pray to God and find favour with Him; they will see His face with joy, and He will restore their righteousness.
They will sing to others and say, ‘I have sinned and perverted what was right, but I did not receive what I deserved.
He has delivered my soul from descending into the pit, and my life shall behold the light.’
“Indeed, God does all these things for a person—twice, even three times—
to bring back their soul from the pit, that they may be enlightened with the light of life.
“Listen carefully, Job, and hear me; remain silent, and I will speak.
If you have anything to say, respond to me; speak, for I wish to justify you.
But if not, then heed my words; stay silent, and I will impart wisdom to you.”
Then Elihu continued and spoke:
“Hear my words, you wise men, and listen to me, you men of understanding.
For just as the palate tastes food, so the ear discerns words.
Let us choose for ourselves what is right; let us discern together what is good.
For Job has said, ‘I am righteous, but God has denied me justice.
Though I am without fault, I am considered a liar; my wound is incurable, though I am guiltless.’
What man is like Job, who drinks derision like water,
who associates with evildoers and walks with the wicked?
For he has said, ‘There is no benefit to a man in trying to please God.’
“Therefore, listen to me, you men of understanding. It is far from God to do evil, and from the Almighty to do wrong.
He repays a person according to their deeds, and brings upon them what their conduct deserves.
It is unimaginable that God would act wickedly, or that the Almighty would pervert justice.
Who gave Him authority over the earth? Who placed the whole world in His care?
If He were to gather His spirit and breath to Himself,
all humanity would perish together, and mankind would return to dust.
“If you are wise, pay attention to this; heed the sound of my words.
Can one who hates justice govern? Will you condemn the righteous and mighty One?
Is He not the One who declares to kings, ‘You are of no account,’ and to nobles, ‘You are corrupt,’
who shows no partiality to princes and does not favour the wealthy over the poor, for they are all the work of His hands?
In a moment they die, in the middle of the night; people are shaken and pass away, and the powerful are removed without human hand.
“For His eyes are on the ways of mortals; He sees their every step.
There is no darkness or deep shadow where evildoers can hide themselves.
God does not need to examine people further, that they should come before Him for judgment.
Without inquiry, He shatters the mighty and sets others in their place.
Because He observes their actions, He overthrows them in the night and they are crushed.
He strikes them for their wickedness in a public place,
because they turned away from following Him and had no regard for any of His ways.
They caused the cry of the poor to come before Him, so that He heard the cry of the afflicted.
When He remains silent, who can condemn Him? If He hides His face, who can see Him? He is over both nation and individual alike,
to prevent the godless from ruling and laying snares for the people.
“For has anyone said to God, ‘I have endured punishment; I will offend no more;
show me what I do not see; if I have done wrong, I will not repeat it’?
Should God repay on your terms because you reject His? You must choose, not I; therefore, declare what you know.
Men of understanding will say to me, and a wise man who hears me will say,
‘Job speaks without knowledge; his words lack wisdom.’
Oh, that Job might be thoroughly tested, for answering like a wicked man!
For he adds rebellion to his sin; he claps his hands among us and increases his words against God.”
Then Elihu continued and spoke:
“Do you consider this to be just? You claim, ‘My righteousness surpasses God’s.’
For you ask, ‘What benefit is it to me? What do I gain more than if I had sinned?’
I shall respond to you and your companions.
Observe the heavens and see; look at the clouds, they are far above you.
If you sin, what effect does it have on Him? If your transgressions are many, how do they affect Him?
If you are righteous, what can you offer Him, or what does He want from your hand?
Your wickedness affects only humans like yourself, and your righteousness impacts only fellow people.
“People cry out under the weight of oppression; they seek relief from the power of the mighty.
Yet no one asks, ‘Where is God my Maker, who provides songs in the night,
who teaches us more than the beasts of the earth and makes us wiser than the birds of the sky?’
They cry out, but He does not answer because of the arrogance of the wicked.
Indeed, God will not heed an empty plea; the Almighty pays no attention to it.
How much less will He listen when you claim you do not see Him, that your case is before Him, and you must wait for Him.
And now, because He has not punished in His anger, nor has He regarded wrongdoing,
So Job speaks with empty words; he multiplies words without understanding.”
Elihu continued speaking and said:
“Bear with me a little while longer, and I will demonstrate that there is more to be spoken on God's behalf.
I will bring forth my knowledge from afar and attribute righteousness to my Maker.
Rest assured, my words are not false; one perfect in knowledge is with you.
Behold, God is mighty and does not reject anyone; He is mighty in strength and understanding.
He does not preserve the wicked, but upholds the rights of the afflicted.
He does not avert His gaze from the righteous; He seats them with kings on the throne and exalts them forever.
If they are bound in chains and caught in cords of affliction,
then He declares to them their deeds and their transgressions, that they have acted arrogantly.
He opens their ears to instruction and commands them to turn from iniquity.
If they heed and serve Him, they will spend their days in prosperity and their years in happiness.
Yet if they do not listen, they will meet their end by the sword and pass away without understanding.
The godless in heart harbour anger; they do not call for help when He binds them.
They die in their youth, and their life ends among the shrine's male prostitutes.
He rescues the afflicted in their misery and opens their ears in times of oppression.
Indeed, He is drawing you from the jaws of distress to a broad place without constraint, and your table is laden with rich food.
But now you are filled with the judgment due to the wicked; judgment and justice have taken hold of you.
Beware that wrath does not entice you to scorn, and do not let a large ransom turn you aside.
Will your wealth or the exertion of your strength keep you from distress?
Do not long for the night, when people are taken from their places.
Be wary of turning to evil, which you seem to prefer over affliction.
Behold, God is supreme in His power; who can instruct like Him?
Who has appointed His path for Him, or who has said, ‘You have done wrong’?
Remember to extol His work, which people have sung in praise.
All humanity has observed it; mankind gazes upon it from afar.
Behold, God is great beyond our understanding; the number of His years is unsearchable.
For He draws up the drops of water, which distil as rain from the celestial streams,
the clouds pour down their moisture, and abundant showers fall upon mankind.
Can anyone comprehend the spreading of the clouds, the thunder from His pavilion?
Behold, He scatters His lightning around Him and covers the depths of the sea.
For by these He governs the nations and provides food in abundance.
He fills His hands with lightning and commands it to strike its target.
His thunder announces the storm; even the cattle are aware of its approach.
“At this, my heart quakes, And leaps from its place.
Hear attentively the thunder of His voice, And the rumbling that issues from His mouth.
Across the whole sky He releases it, And His lightning reaches the ends of the earth.
Following it, a voice roars; He thunders with His majestic voice, And He does not hold back the lightning when His voice is heard.
God thunders marvellously with His voice, Accomplishing great things beyond our understanding.
To the snow He commands, ‘Fall on the earth,’ And to the rain shower, ‘Be strong.’
He seals the hand of every person, So that all people may recognise His work.
Then the animal retreats to its lair And stays in its den.
From the south comes the storm, And from the north the cold.
By the breath of God ice forms, And the expanse of the waters is frozen.
He also fills the clouds with moisture; He scatters the cloud of His lightning.
It shifts direction, turning around by His guidance, That it may accomplish whatever He commands it On the face of the inhabited earth.
Whether for correction, or for His world, Or for mercy, He causes it to happen.
“Hear this, Job: Stand and reflect on the wonders of God.
Do you know how God establishes them, And makes the lightning of His cloud shine?
Do you understand about the hovering of the clouds, The wonders of One who is perfect in knowledge,
You whose garments are warm, When the land is still because of the south wind?
Can you, with Him, spread out the sky, Strong as a cast metal mirror?
“Instruct us what we should say to Him; We cannot present our case due to darkness.
Shall it be reported to Him that I would speak? Or should a person say that he would be overwhelmed?
At present, people do not see the light that is bright in the skies; But the wind has passed and cleared them.
From the north comes golden splendour; Around God is awesome majesty.
The Almighty— we cannot fully comprehend Him; He is exalted in power, And He will not compromise justice and abundant righteousness.
Therefore people revere Him; He does not regard any who are wise in heart.”
Then the Lord spoke to Job from the whirlwind and said:
“Who is this that darkens my counsel with words devoid of knowledge?
Gird yourself like a man; I will question you, and you shall inform me.
Where were you when I established the earth’s foundation? Declare, if you have understanding.
Who determined its measurements? Surely you know! Or who stretched the measuring line upon it?
On what were its bases set, or who laid its cornerstone—
when the morning stars sang in chorus and all the sons of God shouted for joy?
“Who enclosed the sea with doors when it burst forth from the womb,
when I clothed it with clouds and wrapped it in thick darkness,
when I set limits for it and put bars and doors in place,
when I said, ‘This far you may come and no farther; here is where your proud waves shall stop’?
“Have you ever commanded the morning, or shown the dawn its place,
that it might seize the edges of the earth and shake the wicked out of it?
The earth takes form like clay under a seal; its features stand out like a garment.
The light is withheld from the wicked, and the uplifted arm is broken.
“Have you journeyed to the springs of the sea or walked in the depths of the ocean?
Have the secrets of death been shown to you? Have you seen the gates of the shadowy realm?
Have you comprehended the vast expanses of the earth? Declare, if you know all this.
Where is the way to the dwelling of light? And where does darkness reside,
that you may take it to its territory, and discern the paths to its abode?
Surely you must know, for you were born by then, and your days are many!
“Have you entered the treasuries of the snow or seen the reserves of the hail,
which I have reserved for times of trouble, for days of war and battle?
What is the way to the place where the light is divided, or the east wind scattered over the earth?
Who has carved a channel for the torrents of rain, and a path for the thunderbolt,
to water a land that is uninhabited, a deserted wilderness,
to satisfy the desolate wasteland and cause the grass to sprout?
Does the rain have a source? Who is the parent of the drops of dew?
From whose womb does the ice come? Who gives birth to the frost from the heavens
when the waters harden like stone, when the surface of the deep is frozen?
“Can you bind the chains of the Pleiades, or loosen the cords of Orion?
Can you bring forth the constellations in their seasons, or guide the Bear with her cubs?
Are you familiar with the laws of the heavens? Can you establish their rule over the earth?
“Can you call to the clouds and cover yourself with a deluge of water?
Do you send the lightning bolts on their way? Do they present themselves to you, saying, ‘Here we are’?
Who has placed wisdom within the heart, or given understanding to the mind?
Who can count the clouds by wisdom, or tip over the water jars of the heavens
when the dust becomes solid and the clods stick together?
“Can you hunt the prey for the lioness and satisfy the hunger of the young lions
when they crouch in their dens or lie in wait in their lairs?
Who provides sustenance for the raven when its young cry out to God and wander about without food?
“Do you know the moment when the mountain goats give birth? Do you watch when the deer bring forth their young?
Can you count the months they fulfil, Or do you know the time they give birth?
They kneel down, they deliver their young, They get rid of their labour pains.
Their offspring grow strong, maturing in the open field; They depart and do not return to them.
Who set the wild donkey free? And who released the swift donkey from its bonds,
To whom I gave the wilderness as a home, And the salt land as his dwelling place?
He mocks the chaos of the city, He does not hear the shouting of the taskmaster.
He roams the mountains for his pasture, And seeks out every green thing.
Will the wild ox be willing to serve you, Or will he spend the night at your feeding trough?
Can you tie down the wild ox in a furrow with ropes, Or will he plough the valleys after you?
Will you trust him because his strength is great, And leave your labour to him?
Will you rely on him to bring back your grain, And gather it from your threshing floor?
“The wings of the ostrich wave joyfully, With the pinion and feathers of affection,
For she leaves her eggs on the ground, And lets them warm in the dust,
And she is unaware that a foot may crush them, Or that a wild animal might trample them.
She treats her young harshly, as if they were not hers; Though her labour is in vain, she is unconcerned,
Because God has deprived her of wisdom, And has not granted her a share of understanding.
When she takes to flight, She mocks the horse and its rider.
“Do you endow the horse with strength? Do you adorn his neck with a flowing mane?
Do you make him leap like a locust? His majestic snorting is terrifying.
He paws in the valley, exulting in his strength; He charges into the fray.
He scoffs at fear and is undaunted; And he does not turn back from the sword.
The quiver clatters against him, The flashing spear and javelin.
He races over the ground with a roar and fury, And he does not stand still at the sound of the trumpet.
Whenever the trumpet sounds he exclaims, ‘Aha!’ And he perceives the battle from afar, And the thunder of the captains and the war cry.
“Is it through your wisdom that the hawk soars, Stretching his wings toward the south?
Is it by your command that the eagle ascends, And makes his nest on high?
He dwells and spends his nights on the cliff, On the rocky crag, an inaccessible place.
From there he searches for prey; His eyes observe it from afar.
His young ones also feast on blood; And where the slain are, there he is.”
Then the Lord addressed Job,
“Will the one who finds fault contend with the Almighty? Let the one who challenges God respond.”
Job responded to the Lord, and said,
“Behold, I am of little account; how can I answer You? I will place my hand over my mouth.
I have spoken once, and I will not reply; twice, and I will add nothing further.”
Then the Lord spoke to Job from the whirlwind,
“Prepare yourself like a man; I will question you, and you shall inform me.
Will you indeed annul my judgement? Will you condemn me to justify yourself?
Do you possess strength like that of God, and can your voice resound with power like His?
Adorn yourself with grandeur and dignity, and clothe yourself with honour and majesty.
Release the fury of your wrath, and look at all who are proud and humble them.
Look at all who are arrogant and bring them low, and crush the wicked where they stand.
Bury them in the dust together; imprison them in the hidden place.
Then I will acknowledge to you that your own right hand can deliver you.
Behold, Behemoth, which I made along with you; it eats grass like an ox.
See the strength in its loins, and the power in the muscles of its belly.
Its tail sways like a cedar; the sinews of its thighs are closely bound.
Its bones are like tubes of bronze, its limbs like bars of iron.
It is the foremost of the works of God; its Maker can approach it with His sword.
The mountains yield food for it, and all the wild animals play nearby.
Beneath the lotus plants it rests, concealed among the reeds in the marsh.
The lotuses provide it with shade; the willows of the brook surround it.
Though a river rages, it is not alarmed; it remains confident, even if the Jordan surges against its mouth.
Can anyone capture it when it is alert, or pierce its nose with snares?
“Can you draw out Leviathan with a fishhook, And hold down his tongue with a cord?
Can you place a cord through his nose, And pierce his jaw with a hook?
Will he plead with you repeatedly, Or will he speak to you with gentle words?
Will he make an agreement with you? Will you take him as a servant for all time?
Will you play with him as with a bird, Or bind him for your young maidens?
Will the traders haggle over him? Will they divide him among the merchants?
Can you cover his skin with harpoons, Or his head with fishing spears?
Place your hand upon him. Recall the battle; you will not attempt it again!
Behold, your expectation is in vain; Will you be cast down even at the sight of him?
No one is so fearless that he dares to rouse him; Who then is he who can stand before Me?
Who has given to Me that I should repay him? Whatever is under the entire heaven is Mine.
“I will not be silent about his limbs, Or his mighty strength, or his graceful form.
Who can strip off his outer covering? Who can penetrate his double armour?
Who can open his mouth? His teeth inspire awe.
His strong scales are his pride, Sealed tightly together.
Each one is so close to the next That no air can pass between them.
They are joined one to another; They cling together and cannot be separated.
His sneezes emit flashes of light, And his eyes are like the rays of dawn.
From his mouth come burning torches; Sparks of fire shoot forth.
Smoke pours from his nostrils As from a boiling pot and burning reeds.
His breath kindles coals, And a flame issues from his mouth.
Power is in his neck, And fear moves before him.
The folds of his flesh are tightly joined, Firm and immovable upon him.
His heart is hard as a stone, Indeed, as hard as a lower millstone.
When he rises up, the mighty are terrified; Because of the crashing they are bewildered.
The sword that strikes him does not prevail, Nor the spear, the dart, or the javelin.
He considers iron as straw, Bronze as decayed wood.
The arrow cannot make him flee; Slingstones are like chaff to him.
Clubs are regarded as chaff; He laughs at the clatter of the javelin.
His underparts are like sharp shards of pottery; He spreads himself like a threshing sledge on the mire.
He makes the depths churn like a pot; He makes the sea like a pot of ointment.
Behind him he leaves a glistening wake; One would think the deep to be grey-haired.
There is nothing on earth like him, A creature made without fear.
He surveys everything that is lofty; He is king over all the proud.”
Then Job responded to the Lord and said,
“I am aware that you are capable of all things, and that no plan of yours can be thwarted.
‘Who is this that obscures counsel without knowledge?’ Indeed, I spoke of things I did not understand, things too marvellous for me to know.
‘Listen now, and I will speak; I will question you, and you will inform me.’
I had heard of you by the hearing of the ear, but now my eyes have witnessed you;
Therefore I retract my words and repent in dust and ashes.”

After the Lord had spoken these words to Job, he said to Eliphaz the Temanite, “My anger burns against you and your two friends, because you have not spoken what is right about me, as my servant Job has.

Now take seven bulls and seven rams and go to my servant Job and offer a burnt offering for yourselves. My servant Job will pray for you, and I will accept his prayer and not deal with you according to your folly, because you have not spoken what is right about me, as my servant Job has.”
So Eliphaz the Temanite, Bildad the Shuhite, and Zophar the Naamathite went and did as the Lord had instructed them; and the Lord accepted Job’s prayer.
The Lord restored Job’s fortunes when he prayed for his friends, and the Lord gave him twice as much as he had before.

Then all his brothers, sisters, and everyone who had known him before came to him and ate with him in his house. They sympathised with him and comforted him over all the misfortunes the Lord had brought upon him, and each one gave him a piece of silver and a gold ring.
The Lord blessed the latter part of Job’s life more than the beginning. He had fourteen thousand sheep, six thousand camels, a thousand yoke of oxen, and a thousand donkeys.
He was blessed with seven sons and three daughters.
He named the first daughter Jemimah, the second Keziah, and the third Keren-Happuch.
In all the land, no women were found as beautiful as Job’s daughters, and their father granted them an inheritance alongside their brothers.
After this, Job lived for 140 years; he saw his children and their children to the fourth generation.
