For jealousy enrages a man, and he will show no mercy on the day of vengeance.
He will not accept any compensation; no matter how great the bribe, he will refuse it.
My son, preserve my words and treasure my commandments within you.
Keep my commandments and live; guard my teachings as the apple of your eye.
Bind them upon your fingers; inscribe them on the tablet of your heart.
Say to wisdom, “You are my sister,” and call understanding your close friend,
so they may protect you from the adulterous woman, from the foreign woman who flatters with her words.
For at the window of my house I looked out through the lattice,
and I saw among the simple, I noticed among the youths, a young man lacking sense,
passing along the street near her corner, taking the path to her house
at twilight, in the evening, in the dark of night.
And behold, a woman comes to meet him, dressed like a prostitute and wily of heart.
She is loud and rebellious; her feet do not remain at home;
now in the streets, now in the squares, she lurks at every corner.
She seizes him and kisses him, and with a brazen face she says:
“I have fulfilled my vows today, and I have food from my peace offerings at home.
So I came out to meet you, to seek you earnestly, and I have found you!
I have adorned my bed with coverings, with coloured linens from Egypt.
I have scented my bed with myrrh, aloes, and cinnamon.
Come, let us drink deeply of love until morning; let us delight ourselves with caresses.
For my husband is away; he has embarked on a long journey.
He has taken a bag of money with him and will not return until the full moon.”
With her many persuasions she entices him; with her smooth lips she seduces him.
Suddenly he follows her as an ox goes to the slaughter, or as a deer stepping into a trap,
until an arrow pierces his liver; as a bird rushes into a snare, not knowing it will cost him his life.
So now, my sons, listen to me, and give heed to what I say.
Do not let your heart turn to her ways, do not wander into her paths.
For she has brought down many victims; her slain are a vast multitude.
Her house is a highway to the grave, descending to the chambers of death.
Does not wisdom call out, and understanding raise her voice?
On the heights along the way, where the paths meet, she takes her stand;
Beside the gates leading into the city, at the entrance, she cries out:
“To you, O people, I call, and my voice is to all mankind.
You who are simple, gain prudence; you who are foolish, understand wisdom.
Listen, for I speak of noble things; the opening of my lips will reveal what is right.
My mouth speaks truth; wickedness is an abomination to my lips.
All the words of my mouth are righteous; there is nothing crooked or perverse in them.
To the discerning, they are all straightforward; to those who find knowledge, they are right.
Accept my instruction instead of silver, and knowledge rather than choice gold.
For wisdom is more valuable than rubies, and nothing you desire can equal her.
“I, wisdom, live with prudence, and I possess knowledge and discretion.
To fear the Lord is to hate evil; I hate pride, arrogance, the evil way, and perverse speech.
Sound advice is mine and true wisdom; I am understanding, strength is mine.
Through me, kings exercise their rule, and rulers make their fair decrees;
by me princes govern, and nobles—all who judge rightly.
I cherish those who love me, and those who earnestly seek me will find me.
With me are riches and honour, enduring wealth and righteousness.
My fruit is better than gold, even pure gold; what I yield surpasses choice silver.
I follow the path of righteousness, in the midst of the ways of justice,
bestowing wealth on those who love me, and filling their treasuries.
“The Lord created me as the first of His works, before His deeds of old.
From eternity I was established, from the beginning, when the world came to be.
Before the ocean depths existed, I was brought forth, before there were springs overflowing with water;
before the mountains were created, before the hills, I was brought forth;
before He made the earth or its fields or any of the dust of the world.
I was there when He set the heavens in place, when He inscribed a circle on the face of the deep,
when He made firm the skies above and fixed securely the fountains of the deep,
when He set a boundary for the sea so that the waters would not overstep His command, and when He marked out the foundations of the earth.
Then I was beside Him, as a master craftsman; I was His delight day after day, rejoicing always in His presence,
finding joy in His creation and taking pleasure in humanity.
“So now, my children, listen to me; blessed are those who follow my ways.
Listen to my instruction and be wise; do not neglect it.
Fortunate is the person who listens to me, watching daily at my gates, waiting at my doorposts.
For whoever finds me discovers life and receives favour from the Lord.
But whoever fails to find me harms themselves; all who hate me love death.”
Wisdom has constructed her dwelling; she has carved out her seven pillars.
She has prepared her feast, she has blended her wine; she has also arranged her table.
She has dispatched her servants, and she calls out from the highest places of the city,
“Let anyone who is simple turn in here!” To those who lack sense she says,
“Come, partake of my food and drink the wine I have mixed.
Forsake your foolishness and live; walk in the path of insight.”
He who corrects a scoffer invites dishonour; he who rebukes the wicked receives insults.
Do not rebuke a scoffer, lest they hate you; rebuke the wise, and they will love you.
Instruct the wise and they will grow wiser still; teach the righteous and they will enhance their learning.
Reverence for the Lord is the foundation of wisdom, and understanding comes from knowing the Holy One.
For through wisdom your days will be multiplied, and years will be added to your life.
If you are wise, your wisdom benefits you; if you scoff, you alone will bear it.
Folly is a boisterous woman; she is naive and knows nothing.
She sits at the entrance of her house, on a seat in the high places of the city,
calling out to those who pass by, while they are going on their way,
“Let anyone who is simple turn in here!” And to those who lack sense she says,
“Stolen water is sweet, and bread eaten in secret is delightful.”
Yet they are unaware that the dead reside there, that her guests are in the depths of the grave.
These are the sayings of Solomon: A wise son brings joy to his father, but a foolish son causes sorrow to his mother.
Ill-gotten gains do not endure, but righteousness delivers from death.
The Lord will not let the righteous go hungry, but He frustrates the desires of the wicked.
Lazy hands lead to poverty, but diligent hands bring wealth.
He who gathers in the summer is a prudent son, but he who sleeps during harvest is a shameful son.
Blessings crown the head of the righteous, but the mouth of the wicked conceals violence.
The memory of the righteous is a blessing, but the name of the wicked will decay.
Those who are wise in heart accept commands, but those who speak foolishly come to ruin.
Whoever walks in integrity walks securely, but whoever takes crooked paths will be discovered.
He who winks maliciously causes grief, and those who talk foolishly will come to ruin.
The words of the righteous are a source of life, but the speech of the wicked conceals harm.
Hatred stirs up conflict, but love covers all wrongs.
Wisdom is found on the lips of the discerning, but a rod is for the back of one who lacks sense.
The wise accumulate knowledge, but the words of a fool invite ruin.
The wealth of the rich is their fortress, but poverty is the ruin of the poor.
The wages of the righteous lead to life, but the income of the wicked brings punishment.
Whoever listens to discipline shows the way to life, but whoever ignores correction leads others astray.
Those who hide hatred with deceitful words and spread slander are foolish.
When words are many, sin is not absent, but whoever restrains their lips is wise.
The tongue of the righteous is like choice silver, but the heart of the wicked is of little value.
The lips of the righteous nourish many, but those lacking sense perish.
The blessing of the Lord brings wealth, without adding sorrow to it.
A foolish person finds joy in wicked schemes, but one who is wise delights in understanding.
What the wicked dread will overtake them, but the desires of the righteous will be granted.
When the storm has passed, the wicked are no more, but the righteous stand firm forever.
As vinegar to the teeth and smoke to the eyes, so is a sluggard to those who send him.
Reverence for the Lord prolongs life, but the years of the wicked are cut short.
The hope of the righteous is joy, but the expectations of the wicked will perish.
The way of the Lord is a refuge for the blameless, but it is ruin for those who do evil.
The righteous will remain secure, but the wicked will not stay in the land.
The words of the righteous bear the fruit of wisdom, but a perverse tongue will be silenced.
The lips of the righteous know what is favourable, but the mouth of the wicked only what is perverse.
The Lord abhors false scales, but accurate weights are His pleasure.
When pride arrives, disgrace follows, but with the humble there is wisdom.
The integrity of the upright guides them, but the treachery of the unfaithful leads to their ruin.
Riches are futile on the day of wrath, but righteousness delivers from death.
The righteousness of the blameless clears their path, but the wicked stumble by their own wickedness.
The righteousness of the upright rescues them, but the treacherous are ensnared by their own greed.
When the wicked die, their hopes perish, and the promise of their might comes to nothing.
The righteous are saved from trouble, and it befalls the wicked instead.
With their mouths the godless ruin their neighbours, but through knowledge the righteous find deliverance.
When the righteous prosper, the city rejoices; when the wicked perish, there is celebration.
A city is exalted by the blessing of the upright, but it is destroyed by the words of the wicked.
Whoever belittles their neighbour lacks judgement, but a person of understanding remains silent.
A gossip betrays a confidence, but a trustworthy person keeps a matter concealed.
Without guidance a nation falls, but victory comes through many counsellors.
Whoever guarantees a stranger will suffer for it, but whoever avoids such pledges is secure.
A kind- hearted woman gains honour, but ruthless men gain only riches.
A merciful person benefits themselves, but the cruel bring harm upon themselves.
A wicked person earns deceptive wages, but the one who sows righteousness receives a true reward.
The steadfast in righteousness attain life, but those who pursue evil meet death.
The Lord detests those with perverse hearts, but He delights in the blameless.
Rest assured, the wicked will not go unpunished, but the righteous will be delivered.
As a gold ring in the snout of a pig, so is a beautiful woman who lacks discretion.
The desires of the righteous end in good, but the expectations of the wicked result in wrath.
One person gives freely, yet gains more; another withholds unduly, and comes to poverty.
A generous person will prosper, and whoever refreshes others will themselves be refreshed.
People curse the one who hoards grain, but blessing is upon the one willing to sell.
Whoever seeks good finds favour, but evil comes to those who pursue it.
Those who trust in their riches will fall, but the righteous will flourish like a green leaf.
Whoever brings trouble upon their family will inherit only wind, and the fool will serve the wise.
The fruit of the righteous is a tree of life, and the wise save lives.
If the righteous are rewarded on earth, how much more the wicked and the sinner!
Whoever loves discipline loves knowledge, but whoever despises rebuke is foolish.
A good person gains favour from the Lord, but He condemns those who devise evil.
No one is established by wickedness, but the root of the righteous remains unshaken.
A wife of noble character is her husband’s crown, but a wife who brings shame is like decay in his bones.
The thoughts of the righteous are just, but the advice of the wicked is deceitful.
The words of the wicked lie in ambush for blood, but the speech of the upright rescues them.
The wicked are overthrown and vanish, but the house of the righteous stands firm.
A person is praised according to their insight, but one with a perverse mind is despised.
Better to be of lowly status and have a servant, than to pretend to be important and lack food.
The righteous care for the life of their animals, but the kindest acts of the wicked are cruel.
Those who work their land will have plenty of food, but those who chase worthless pursuits lack sense.
The wicked desire the plunder of evildoers, but the root of the righteous bears fruit.
Evildoers are ensnared by their sinful talk, but the righteous escape trouble.
From the fruit of their words people are filled with good things, and the work of their hands brings them reward.
The way of a fool seems right to them, but the wise heed advice.
Fools show their annoyance instantly, but the prudent overlook an insult.
An honest witness speaks truthfully, but a false witness speaks deceit.
Rash words pierce like swords, but the tongue of the wise brings healing.
Truthful speech is enduring, but deceitful words are short-lived.
Those who harbour deceit in their hearts plot evil, but those who promote peace experience joy.
The righteous encounter no lasting harm, but the wicked are filled with trouble.
The Lord detests lying lips, but He delights in those who are trustworthy.
The prudent keep their knowledge to themselves, but the heart of fools blurts out folly.
Diligent hands will rule, but laziness leads to forced labour.
Anxiety burdens the heart, but a kind word lifts it up.
The righteous are careful in choosing their friends, but the way of the wicked leads them astray.
The lazy do not roast their game, but the diligent make use of their resources.
In the way of righteousness there is life; along its path there is no death.
A wise son listens to his father’s guidance, but a scoffer does not pay attention to correction.
From the fruit of one's mouth, good things are enjoyed, but the desire of the unfaithful is for violence.
Those who guard their mouth preserve their life, but those who speak rashly come to ruin.
The soul of the lazy craves and gets nothing, but the desires of the diligent are fully satisfied.
The righteous detest falsehood, but the wicked act shamefully and disgustingly.
Righteousness protects the person of integrity, but wickedness overthrows the sinner.
One pretends to be rich and has nothing; another pretends to be poor and has great wealth.
A person's wealth may ransom their life, but the poor hear no rebuke.
The light of the righteous shines brightly, but the lamp of the wicked shall go out.
Pride leads to strife, but wisdom is found in those who accept counsel.
Wealth obtained dishonestly dwindles, but whoever gathers little by little increases it.
Delayed hope can weigh heavily on the heart, but when a desire is fulfilled, it is like a tree of life.
Whoever scorns instruction will suffer for it, but whoever respects a command is rewarded.
The teaching of the wise is a fountain of life, turning one away from the snares of death.
Good judgment gains favour, but the way of the treacherous leads to destruction.
Every prudent person acts with knowledge, but a fool exposes their folly.
A wicked messenger encounters misfortune, but a trustworthy envoy brings healing.
Poverty and shame come to those who disregard discipline, but those who heed correction are honoured.
A longing fulfilled is sweet to the soul, but fools detest turning away from evil.
Keep company with the wise and become wise, for a companion of fools suffers harm.
Trouble pursues sinners, but the righteous are rewarded with prosperity.
A good person leaves an inheritance for their grandchildren, but a sinner’s wealth is stored up for the righteous.
Abundant food is found in the unploughed field of the poor, but it is swept away by injustice.
Whoever spares the rod hates their child, but the one who loves their child is diligent to discipline them.
The righteous eat to their heart's content, but the stomach of the wicked remains hungry.
The wise woman constructs her house, but the foolish one demolishes it with her own hands.
Whoever walks in uprightness fears the Lord, but the devious in their ways show contempt for Him.
In the mouth of the foolish is a rod for their back, but the lips of the wise will safeguard them.
Where there are no oxen, the manger is clean, but abundant harvests come from the strength of an ox.
A truthful witness does not lie, but a false witness speaks deceit.
A mocker seeks wisdom and finds none, but knowledge is easily found by those with understanding.
Depart from the presence of a fool, for you will not find words of knowledge on their lips.
The wisdom of the prudent is to understand their way, but the folly of fools is deceit.
Fools make light of making amends for sin, but goodwill is found among the upright.
The heart alone knows the extent of its own bitterness, and no stranger can fully share its joy.
The dwelling of the wicked will be brought down, but the abode of the upright will flourish.
There is a way that seems right to a person, but in the end, it leads to death.
Even when laughing, the heart may be in pain, and the conclusion of joy may be sorrow.
The faithless will be filled with their own ways, but a good person will be satisfied with theirs.
The simple believe anything, but the prudent consider their steps.
A wise person is careful and avoids evil, but a fool is reckless and overconfident.
A quick-tempered person acts foolishly, and one who devises evil schemes is despised.
The naive acquire foolishness, but those who are prudent are adorned with knowledge.
Evildoers will bow down before the good, and the wicked at the gates of the righteous.
The poor are shunned even by their neighbour, but the rich have many friends.
It is a sin to despise one's neighbour, but blessed is the one who is kind to the poor.
Do not those who plot evil go astray? But kindness and truth come to those who plan what is good.
In all diligent work there is profit, but idle talk leads only to poverty.
The wealth of the wise is their crown, but the folly of fools is simply folly.
A truthful witness saves lives, but one who speaks lies is deceitful.
In the fear of the Lord there is strong confidence, and for their children it will be a refuge.
The fear of the Lord is a fountain of life, turning one away from the snares of death.
The multitude of a nation is a king's honour, but a prince is undone when he has no followers.
Whoever is slow to anger has great understanding, but one who is quick-tempered displays folly.
A tranquil heart gives life to the body, but envy rots the bones.
Whoever oppresses the poor shows contempt for their Maker, but whoever is kind to the needy honours Him.
When calamity comes, the wicked are brought down, but the righteous have a refuge even in death.
Wisdom rests in the heart of the discerning, but even among fools, she is made known.
Righteousness exalts up a nation, but sin brings disgrace to any people.
A king delights in a servant who acts wisely, but his anger is towards one who acts shamefully.
A gentle answer deflects anger, but a harsh word incites fury.
The tongue of the wise enhances knowledge, but the mouth of fools spills folly.
The eyes of the Lord are in every place, observing the wicked and the good.
A soothing tongue is a tree of life, but a twisted tongue crushes the spirit.
A fool rejects a parent’s discipline, but whoever accepts correction is wise.
The house of the righteous holds great wealth, but the income of the wicked brings trouble.
The lips of the wise spread knowledge, but the hearts of fools are not right.
The sacrifice of the wicked is detestable to the Lord, but the prayer of the upright delights Him.
The Lord finds the way of the wicked distasteful, but He loves those who follow righteousness.
Severe discipline awaits anyone who strays from the path; one who hates correction will die.
Sheol and Abaddon lie open before the Lord; how much more so the hearts of mankind!
A mocker resents correction and will not seek the wise.
A joyful heart makes a cheerful face, but a broken heart crushes the spirit.
The discerning heart seeks knowledge, but the mouth of fools indulges in folly.
All the days of the oppressed are difficult, but a cheerful heart enjoys a continual feast.
Better is a little with the fear of the Lord than great wealth with turmoil.
Better is a small serving of vegetables with love than a fattened ox with hatred.
A hot-tempered person stirs up conflict, but one slow to anger calms a dispute.
The way of the sluggard is like a hedge of thorns, but the path of the upright is a highway.
A wise child brings joy to their father, but a foolish person shows disregard for their mother.
Foolishness brings joy to one who lacks sense, but a person of understanding walks a straight path.
Plans fail without counsel, but with many advisers they succeed.
A person finds joy in an apt reply, and how delightful is a timely word!
The path of life leads upwards for the prudent, to keep them from descending to Sheol.
The Lord will tear down the house of the proud, but He will protect the boundary of the widow.
The Lord finds evil plans detestable, but gracious words are pure.
The greedy bring trouble to their household, but the one who hates bribes will live.
The heart of the righteous considers how to answer, but the mouth of the wicked pours out evil.
The Lord keeps His distance from the wicked, but He hears the prayer of the righteous.
Bright eyes bring joy to the heart; good news refreshes the bones.
Whoever listens to life-giving correction will dwell among the wise.
Those who neglect discipline despise themselves, but whoever heeds correction gains understanding.
The fear of the Lord is the instruction for wisdom, and before honour comes humility.
The plans of the heart belong to humans, but the response of the tongue is from the Lord.
All a person’s ways are pure in their own eyes, but the Lord weighs the motives.
Commit your actions to the Lord, and He will establish your plans.
The Lord has made everything for its own purpose, even the wicked for the day of trouble.
The Lord detests all who are proud in heart; be assured, they will not go unpunished.
Through mercy and truth, sin is atoned for; through the fear of the Lord, evil is avoided.
When the Lord is pleased with a person's ways, he makes even their enemies live at peace with them.
Better a little with righteousness than great gain with injustice.
In their hearts, humans plan their course, but the Lord directs their steps.
A divine decision is on the lips of a king; his mouth should not betray justice.
Honest scales and balances are the Lord’s; all the weights in the bag are his concern.
Kings detest wrongdoing, for a throne is established by righteousness.
Righteous lips are the delight of kings, and they love those who speak what is right.
A king’s wrath is a messenger of death, but a wise person will appease it.
In the light of a king’s face is life, and his favour is like a cloud bringing spring rain.
How much better to acquire wisdom than gold, and to gain understanding is to be chosen above silver.
The highway of the upright turns away from evil; those who guard their way preserve their life.
Pride precedes destruction, and a haughty spirit before a fall.
It is better to be humble in spirit with the oppressed than to share spoils with the proud.
Whoever pays attention to instruction will find good, and blessed is the one who trusts in the Lord.
The wise in heart are called discerning, and sweetness of speech increases persuasiveness.
Understanding is a fountain of life to those who have it, but the folly of fools brings punishment.
The heart of the wise makes their mouth prudent, and their lips promote learning.
Pleasant words are like a honeycomb, sweet to the soul and healing to the bones.
There is a way that seems right to a person, but its end leads to death.
A labourer’s appetite works for them; their hunger drives them on.
A worthless person plots evil, and their words are like a scorching fire.
A perverse person stirs up conflict, and a slanderer separates close friends.
A violent person entices their neighbour and leads them onto a harmful path.
Whoever winks with their eye is plotting perversity; whoever compresses their lips brings evil to pass.
Grey hair is a crown of splendour; it is found in the way of righteousness.
Better a patient person than a warrior, one who controls their spirit than one who captures a city.
The lot is cast into the lap, yet its every decision comes from the Lord.
Better is a dry crust with peace than a house full of feasting with conflict.
A wise servant will govern over a disgraceful son and will share the inheritance among the family.
The crucible is for silver and the furnace for gold, but the Lord examines hearts.
An evildoer listens to deceitful lips; a liar heeds a harmful tongue.
Whoever mocks the poor insults their Maker; whoever rejoices at calamity will not go unpunished.
Grandchildren are the pride of their grandparents, and the honour of ones children is found in their parents.
Eloquent speech is unfitting for a fool, much less lying lips for a leader.
A bribe is like a charm to the one who gives it; wherever he turns, he succeeds.
Whoever covers an offence promotes love, but whoever repeats the matter separates close friends.
A reprimand penetrates more deeply into a discerning person than a hundred lashes into a fool.
A rebellious person seeks only evil; therefore a cruel messenger will be sent against him.
Better to meet a bear robbed of her cubs than a fool in his folly.
Evil will not depart from the house of one who repays good with evil.
The start of a quarrel is like releasing water; so abandon the dispute before it breaks out.
Justifying the wicked and condemning the righteous are both detestable to the Lord.
Why should a fool have money to buy wisdom when he has no understanding?
A friend loves at all times, and a sibling is there for times of adversity.
One lacking sense shakes hands in pledge and becomes surety for a neighbour.
Whoever loves a quarrel loves sin; whoever raises his doorway seeks destruction.
One with a perverse heart finds no good, and one with a deceitful tongue falls into trouble.
To have a fool for a child brings sorrow; there is no joy for the parent of a fool.
A cheerful heart is good medicine, but a broken spirit dries up the bones.
A wicked person accepts a bribe in secret to pervert the course of justice.
Wisdom is in the presence of one who has understanding, but the eyes of a fool wander to the ends of the earth.
A foolish son brings grief to his father and sorrow to the mother who gave him birth.
It is not good to punish the innocent, nor to flog the upright for their integrity.
One who has knowledge uses words sparingly, and one with a calm spirit is a person of understanding.
Even a fool is considered wise if he remains silent, and discerning if he holds his tongue.
A person who isolates themselves seeks their own desires; they quarrel against all sound judgment.
Fools take no pleasure in understanding, but only in expressing their own thoughts.
When wickedness arrives, so does contempt, and with dishonour comes reproach.
The words from a person's mouth are deep waters; the wellspring of wisdom is a flowing brook.
It is not good to show favouritism to the wicked or to deny justice to the righteous.
The lips of a fool bring them into conflict, and their mouth invites a beating.
A fool's mouth is their ruin, and their lips are a trap for their soul.
The words of a gossip are like choice morsels; they go down to the innermost parts.
One who is slack in their work is a sibling to one who destroys.
The name of the Lord is a strong tower; the righteous run to it and find safety.
The wealth of the rich is their fortified city; they perceive it as an unscalable wall.
Before destruction, a person's heart is proud, but humility precedes honour.
To respond before listening is foolishness and brings shame.
The spirit of a person can sustain them in sickness, but a broken spirit who can endure?
The discerning heart acquires knowledge, and the ears of the wise seek it out.
A gift paves the way for the giver and brings them before the great.
The first to present their case seems right, until another comes and examines them.
Casting lots ends disputes and separates powerful opponents.
An offended brother is more unyielding than a fortified city, and disputes are like the barred gates of a citadel.
From the fruit of their mouth a person's stomach is filled; with the yield of their lips they are satisfied.
The tongue holds the power of life and death, and those who love it will eat its fruit.
Whoever finds a wife finds a good thing and receives favour from the Lord.
The poor plead for mercy, but the rich respond harshly.
One with too many friends may come to ruin, but there is a friend who sticks closer than a brother.
Better is a poor person who walks in integrity than one who is perverse in speech and is a fool.
Desire without knowledge is not good, and one who hurries his steps misses the way.
A person’s own folly leads to ruin, yet his heart rages against the Lord.
Wealth brings many companions, but a poor person finds himself distanced from friends.
A false witness will not go unpunished, and whoever spreads lies will not escape.
Many seek the favour of a generous person, and everyone is a friend to one who gives gifts.
All the relatives of a poor person shun him; how much more do his friends avoid him! Though he pursues them with pleading, they are nowhere to be found.
The one who acquires wisdom loves his own soul; the one who cherishes understanding will find good.
A false witness will not go unpunished, and whoever spreads lies will meet their end.
Opulence is not suitable for a fool; even less so for a servant to govern over nobles.
A person’s wisdom makes him slow to anger; it is to his glory to overlook an offence.
A king’s wrath is like the roaring of a lion, but his favour is like dew on the grass.
A foolish child is a father’s ruin, and the quarrels of a wife are like a constant dripping.
Property and riches are passed down from parents, but a wise wife is from the Lord.
Laziness brings on deep sleep, and a lazy person will suffer hunger.
Whoever keeps the commandment keeps his life, but whoever is careless of conduct will die.
Whoever is kind to the poor lends to the Lord, and He will repay him for his good deed.
Discipline your children while there is hope, and do not be a willing party to their death.
A hot-tempered person must pay the penalty; if you rescue him, you will have to do it again.
Listen to advice and accept discipline, so that you may be wise for the rest of your days.
Many are the plans in a person’s heart, but it is the Lord’s purpose that will stand.
What is desirable in a person is his kindness; it is better to be poor than a liar.
Reverence for the Lord leads to life; then one rests content, untouched by trouble.
The sluggard buries his hand in the bowl but will not even bring it back to his mouth.
Strike a mocker, and the simple will learn prudence; rebuke the discerning, and they will gain knowledge.
He who robs his father and drives out his mother is a shameful and disgraceful son.
If you cease to heed instruction, you will stray from the words of knowledge.
A worthless witness mocks at justice, and the mouth of the wicked gulps down wrongdoing.
Judgments are ready for mockers, and blows for the backs of fools.
Wine is a mocker, and strong drink a brawler; whoever is led astray by it is not wise.
A king’s wrath is like the roar of a lion; one who provokes him forfeits their life.
To avoid strife is an honour for a person, but any fool is quick to quarrel.
The sluggard does not plough in season; so at harvest time they look and find nothing.
The purposes in a person’s heart are like deep waters, but one with understanding draws them out.
Many proclaim their own steadfast love, but a faithful person who can find?
The righteous walk in integrity; blessed are their children after them.
A king who sits on the throne of judgement winnows out all evil with his eyes.
Who can claim, “I have purified my heart; I am free from my sin”?
Differing weights and differing measures— both are detestable to the Lord.
Even a child is known by their actions, whether their conduct is pure and right.
The ability to hear and see— both are crafted by the Lord.
Do not love sleep, or you will grow poor; open your eyes, and you will have food to spare.
“It’s no good, it’s no good!” says the buyer, but then goes off and boasts.
There is gold and an abundance of jewels, but lips that speak knowledge are a rare treasure.
Take the garment of one who puts up security for a stranger; hold it in pledge if it is for an outsider.
Food gained by deceit is sweet, but afterwards the mouth is full of gravel.
Plans are established by counsel; make war with wise guidance.
A gossip betrays a confidence; therefore, avoid anyone who talks too much.
If someone curses their father or mother, their lamp will be snuffed out in darkness.
An inheritance claimed hastily will not be blessed in the end.
Do not declare, “I will repay evil”; instead, wait for the Lord, and he will deliver you.
Differing weights are an abomination to the Lord, and dishonest scales are not good.
The Lord directs a person’s steps; how then can anyone fully understand their own path?
It is risky to dedicate something impulsively and only later reflect on one’s vows.
A wise king winnows out the wicked and drives the threshing wheel over them.
The human spirit is the lamp of the Lord, searching all the innermost parts.
Love and faithfulness preserve a king; through loyalty his throne is made secure.
The glory of young men is their strength, and the splendour of old men is their grey hair.
Blows and wounds purge away evil, and beatings cleanse the innermost being.
The heart of the king is like streams of water in the hand of the Lord; He directs it wherever He desires.
A person’s way may seem right in their own eyes, but the Lord evaluates the heart.
To act with righteousness and justice is more desirable to the Lord than sacrifice.
Haughty eyes and a proud heart, the lamp of the wicked, are sinful.
The plans of the diligent lead surely to profit, but those who are hasty end in poverty.
Gaining wealth through a lying tongue is a fleeting vapour and a deadly trap.
The violence of the wicked will carry them away, because they refuse to act justly.
The path of the guilty is twisted, but the actions of the pure are straightforward.
Better to live on the corner of a roof than to share a house with a contentious woman.
The soul of the wicked desires evil; they show no mercy to their neighbour.
When a mocker is punished, the simple gain wisdom; when the wise are instructed, they receive knowledge.
The Righteous One considers the house of the wicked and brings them to ruin.
Whoever closes their ears to the cry of the poor will also cry out and not be answered.
A gift given in secret calms anger, and a bribe hidden in the cloak pacifies strong wrath.
The execution of justice brings joy to the righteous but terror to those who do evil.
Whoever strays from the path of understanding will rest in the company of the dead.
Whoever loves pleasure will become poor; whoever loves wine and oil will not become wealthy.
The wicked serve as a ransom for the righteous, and the treacherous for the upright.
Better to live in a desert than with a contentious and irritable woman.
The wise store up precious treasure and oil, but the foolish consume it all.
Whoever seeks righteousness and love discovers life, prosperity, and honour.
A wise person can scale the city of the mighty and bring down the stronghold in which they trust.
Those who guard their mouths and tongues protect themselves from trouble.
The proud and arrogant person, known as “Scoffer,” acts with insolent pride.
The desire of the sluggard will be his downfall, for his hands refuse to work.
All day long he craves, but the righteous give generously without holding back.
The sacrifice of the wicked is detestable, and even more so when brought with evil intent.
A false witness will perish, but the one who listens will speak with endurance.
A wicked person puts on a bold face, but the upright gives careful thought to their ways.
There is no wisdom, no understanding, and no plan that can prevail against the Lord.
The horse is prepared for the day of battle, but victory is of the Lord.
A good name is more to be desired than great wealth; to be esteemed is better than silver or gold.
The rich and the poor share this in common: the Lord is the Maker of them all.
A prudent person sees danger and takes refuge, but the naive continue on and pay the penalty.
The reward of humility and the fear of the Lord is riches, honour, and life.
Thorns and snares lie in the path of the wicked; those who guard their lives will stay far from them.
Train a child in the way he should go, and even when he is old he will not depart from it.
Those with wealth have authority over those with less, and those who borrow are in a position of service to their lenders.
Whoever sows injustice will reap calamity, and the rod of their fury will be broken.
Those who are generous will be blessed, for they share their provisions with those in need.
Expel the scoffer, and strife will depart; quarrels and insults will cease.
One who loves a pure heart and whose speech is gracious will have the king as a friend.
The eyes of the Lord watch over knowledge, but He overthrows the words of the unfaithful.
The lazy person says, “There is a lion outside! I will be killed in the streets!”
The mouth of an adulterous woman is a deep pit; he who is cursed by the Lord will fall into it.
Foolishness is bound up in the heart of a child, but the rod of discipline will drive it far away.
One who oppresses the poor to increase wealth, or gives gifts to the rich, will only come to poverty.
Incline your ear and listen to the words of the wise; apply your heart to my knowledge,
for it is pleasing when you keep them within you and have them ready on your lips.
So that your trust may be in the Lord, I instruct you today, even you.
Have I not provided you with numerous sayings of counsel and knowledge,
to teach you true and reliable words, so that you may give a correct answer to those who sent you?
Do not exploit the poor because they are poor, nor crush the needy in the gate,
for the Lord will take up their cause and will bring about justice.
Do not make friends with a hot-tempered person, nor associate with one easily angered,
or you may learn their ways and find a snare for yourself.
Do not be among those who shake hands in pledge, or among those who become guarantors for debts.
If you have nothing with which to repay, why should your bed be taken from under you?
Do not move an ancient boundary stone set by your ancestors.
Do you see someone skilled in their work?
They will serve before kings; they will not serve before obscure people. When you sit to dine with a ruler, pay careful attention to what is set before you,
and put a knife to your throat if you are prone to great appetite.
Do not desire his delicacies, for that food is deceptive.
Do not exhaust yourself in pursuit of wealth; cease relying on your own understanding.
When you fix your eyes on it, it is gone, for riches will surely sprout wings and fly to the sky like an eagle.
Do not eat the bread of a begrudging host, nor desire his delicacies;
for just as he thinks within himself, so he is. He says to you, “Eat and drink,” but his heart is not truly with you.
You will vomit up the morsel you have eaten and squander your compliments.
Do not speak in the hearing of a fool, for he will scorn the wisdom of your words.
Do not move an ancient boundary stone or encroach upon the fields of the fatherless,
for their Defender is strong; he will take up their cause against you.
Commit yourself to instruction and listen to words of knowledge.
Do not withhold discipline from a child; if you strike them with the rod, they will not die.
Strike them with the rod and rescue their soul from Sheol.
My son, if your heart is wise, then my heart will be glad indeed;
my deepest being will rejoice when your lips speak what is right.
Do not let your heart envy sinners, but always be zealous for the fear of the Lord.
Surely there is a future hope for you, and your hope will not be cut off.
Listen, my son, and be wise, and direct your heart in the right way:
Do not be among those who drink too much wine or gorge themselves on meat,
for drunkards and gluttons will come to poverty, and drowsiness will clothe them in rags.
Pay attention to your father, who gave you life, and do not despise your mother when she is old.
Buy the truth and do not sell it; acquire wisdom, instruction, and understanding.
The father of a righteous child will have great joy; he who fathers a wise son will rejoice in him.
Let your father and mother be glad; let her who gave you birth rejoice.
My son, give me your heart and let your eyes take pleasure in my ways,
for a promiscuous woman is a deep pit, and a wayward wife is a narrow well.
She lurks like a bandit and increases the unfaithful among men.
Who has woe? Who has sorrow? Who has strife? Who has complaints? Who has needless bruises? Who has bloodshot eyes?
Those who linger long over wine, who go to sample mixed wine.
Do not be captivated by wine when it is red, when it sparkles in the cup, when it goes down smoothly;
for in the end it bites like a serpent and stings like a viper.
Your eyes will see peculiar things, and your mind will speak distorted things.
You will be like one sleeping on the high seas, lying on top of the mast.
“They struck me,” you will say, “but I was not hurt! They beat me, but I did not feel it! When will I wake up so I can find another drink?”
Do not envy evil people, nor desire their company;
for their hearts devise violence, and their lips speak of trouble.
Through wisdom a house finds its foundation, and through understanding it becomes firmly set;
through knowledge its rooms are filled with precious and pleasant treasures.
A wise person is strong, and a person of knowledge enhances their strength.
For with wise guidance you can wage war, and victory is achieved through many advisers.
Wisdom is beyond the reach of fools; at the city gate they do not open their mouths.
Whoever plans evil will be known as a schemer.
The plotting of foolishness is sin, and a mocker is detested by people.
If you falter in times of trouble, your strength is small.
Rescue those being led to death; hold back those staggering towards slaughter.
If you claim, “We did not know this,” does not he who weighs the heart perceive it? Does not he who guards your life know it? Will he not repay everyone according to their deeds?
My son, eat honey, for it is good; honey from the comb is sweet to your taste.
Know that wisdom is sweet to your soul; if you find it, there is a future, and your hope will not be cut off.
Do not lie in wait, you wicked person, against the home of the righteous; do not destroy their dwelling place;
for though the righteous fall seven times, they rise again, but the wicked stumble when disaster strikes.
Do not rejoice when your enemy falls, and do not let your heart be glad when they stumble,
lest the Lord take notice and turn his attention away from them.
Do not fret because of evildoers or envy the wicked,
for the evildoer has no future, and the lamp of the wicked will be extinguished.
Fear the Lord and the king, my son, and do not associate with the rebellious,
for their disaster will arise suddenly, and who knows what ruin they can bring?
These also are sayings of the wise: Showing partiality in judgement is not good.
Whoever declares the wicked to be innocent will be cursed by peoples and denounced by nations.
But it will go well with those who convict the guilty, and a rich blessing will come upon them.
An honest sincere response is as pleasing as a gentle kiss.
Prepare your work outside, and make it ready in the field; afterwards, build your house.
Do not testify against your neighbour without cause, and do not deceive with your lips.
Do not say, “I will do to them as they have done to me; I will repay them for what they have done.”
I passed by the field of a sluggard, by the vineyard of someone lacking sense;
and it was overgrown with thorns, the ground was covered with weeds, and the stone wall was in ruins.
I observed and took it to heart; I looked and received instruction.
A bit of sleep, a bit of slumber, a slight folding of the hands to rest—
and poverty will come upon you like a robber, and scarcity like an armed man.
These are further proverbs of Solomon, transcribed by the men of Hezekiah, king of Judah.
It is the glory of God to conceal a matter; the honour of kings is to search out a matter.
As the heavens are high and the earth is deep, so the heart of kings is unsearchable.
Remove the impurities from the silver, and a vessel emerges for the silversmith.
Remove the wicked from the king’s presence, and his throne will be established through righteousness.
Do not exalt yourself in the king’s presence, and do not stand in the place of the great.
It is better for him to say to you, “Come up here,” than for you to be humiliated before the prince whom your eyes have seen.
Do not go hastily to court, for what will you do in the end if your neighbour puts you to shame?
Argue your case with your neighbour, and do not betray another’s confidence,
lest the one who hears it put you to shame, and your reputation be ruined.
A word fitly spoken is like golden apples in silver settings.
Like a gold earring or a fine gold ornament is a wise rebuke to a listening ear.
Like the coolness of snow at harvest time is a faithful messenger to those who send him; he refreshes the soul of his master.
One who boasts of gifts never given is like clouds and wind without rain.
With patience, a ruler may be persuaded, and a gentle tongue can break a bone.
If you find honey, eat only what you need, lest you have too much and vomit.
Let your foot seldom be in your neighbour’s house, lest he become weary of you and hate you.
Like a club, a sword, or a sharp arrow is one who gives false testimony against a neighbour.
Like a bad tooth or a lame foot is reliance on the unfaithful in times of trouble.
Like one who takes away a garment on a cold day, or vinegar on soda, is one who sings songs to a heavy heart.
If your enemy is hungry, provide him with food; if he is thirsty, give him water to drink.
By doing this, you will heap burning coals on his head, and the Lord will reward you.
As the north wind brings rain, so a sly tongue brings an angry face.
Better to live on a corner of the roof than to share a house with a quarrelsome woman.
Good news from a distant land is like cold water to a weary soul.
Like a muddied spring or a polluted well are the righteous who give way before the wicked.
It is not good to eat too much honey, nor is it honourable to seek one’s own glory.
Like a city whose walls are broken down is a person who lacks self-control.
As snow in summer and rain during harvest, so honour does not suit a fool.
Like a fluttering sparrow or a darting swallow, a curse without cause does not alight.
The whip for the horse, the bridle for the donkey, and the stick for the back of fools.
Do not respond to a fool according to his folly, or you will be like him yourself.
Respond to a fool according to his folly, lest he be wise in his own eyes.
Sending a message by a fool is like cutting off one’s feet and drinking violence.
As useless as legs to a lame person is a proverb in the mouth of fools.
Like binding a stone in a sling, so is giving honour to a fool.
Like a thornbush in the hand of a drunkard is a proverb in the mouth of fools.
As an archer who shoots aimlessly is one who hires a fool or any passer-by.
As a dog returns to its vomit, so a fool repeats his folly.
Do you see someone who is wise in their own eyes? There is more hope for a fool than for them.
A sluggard says, “There is a lion in the road, a lion in the streets!”
As a door swings on its hinges, so a sluggard turns on his bed.
A sluggard buries his hand in the dish; he is too weary to bring it back to his mouth.
A sluggard is wiser in his own eyes than seven people who can give a discreet answer.
Like one who seizes a dog by the ears is someone who meddles in a quarrel not their own.
Like a madman who shoots flaming arrows and death,
is one who deceives a neighbour and says, “I was only joking!”
Without wood, a fire goes out; without a gossip, a quarrel subsides.
As charcoal to embers and wood to fire, so is a contentious person to kindle strife.
The words of a gossip are like choice morsels; they go down to the innermost parts.
Like an earthenware vessel coated with silver dross are fervent lips with an evil heart.
Those who are enemies disguise their intentions with their lips, but in their hearts they harbour deceit.
Though their speech is charming, do not believe them, for seven abominations fill their heart.
Their malice may be hidden by deception, but their wickedness will be revealed in the assembly.
Whoever digs a pit will fall into it; if someone rolls a stone, it will return upon them.
A deceitful tongue despises those it harms, and a flattering mouth leads to ruin.
Refrain from boasting about tomorrow, for you do not know what a day may bring.
Allow others to praise you, and not your own mouth; let it be a stranger, and not your own lips.
A stone is heavy and sand is burdensome, but the provocation of a fool is heavier than both.
Anger is cruel and wrath is overwhelming, but who can stand against jealousy?
An open reprimand is better than love that is concealed.
The wounds of a friend can be trusted, but the kisses of an enemy are deceitful.
A person who is full despises honey, but to the hungry even what is bitter tastes sweet.
Like a bird that wanders from its nest, so is a person who leaves their home.
Perfume and oil bring joy to the heart, and the sweetness of a friend comes from their sincere counsel.
Do not forsake your friend or your father's friend, and do not go to your brother's house in the day of your calamity; better is a neighbour who is near than a brother far away.
Be wise, my son, and make my heart glad, so that I may answer those who reproach me.
The prudent see danger and hide themselves, but the simple go on and suffer for it.
Take the garment of one who is surety for a stranger; hold it in pledge when it is for a foreigner.
If anyone blesses their neighbour with a loud voice early in the morning, it will be taken as a curse.
A continual dripping on a rainy day is like a quarrelsome wife.
Trying to restrain her is like restraining the wind or grasping oil with the hand.
Just as iron sharpens iron, so does one person sharpen another.
The one who tends a fig tree will eat its fruit, and whoever looks after their master will be honoured.
As water reflects the face, so the heart of a person reflects the person.
Sheol and Abaddon are never satisfied, and neither are the eyes of a person.
The crucible is for silver and the furnace for gold, but people are tested by the praise they receive.
Though you grind a fool in a mortar, grinding them like grain with a pestle, their folly will not depart from them.
Be sure to know the state of your flocks, and pay attention to your herds;
for wealth does not last forever, nor does a crown endure to all generations.
When the hay is removed and new growth appears, and the herbs of the mountains are gathered in,
the lambs will provide you with clothing, and the goats will bring the price of a field.
And there will be enough goats’ milk for your food, for the food of your household, and sustenance for your maidservants.
The wicked run away even though no one is chasing, but the righteous stand as confident as a lion.
When a land is in rebellion, it has many rulers, but with a person of understanding and knowledge, it endures.
A ruler who oppresses the poor is like a driving rain that leaves no food.
Those who forsake the law praise the wicked, but those who keep it strive against them.
Evildoers do not understand justice, but those who seek the Lord understand it fully.
Better is the poor who walks in integrity than the rich whose ways are crooked.
A discerning son heeds the law, but a companion of gluttons brings shame to his father.
Whoever increases wealth by interest or profit from the poor gathers it for one who will be kind to the poor.
If anyone turns away from listening to the law, even their prayer is detestable.
Whoever leads the upright astray in an evil way will fall into their own pit, but the blameless will inherit good.
The rich are wise in their own eyes, but the poor with understanding sees through them.
When the righteous succeed, there is great celebration; but when the wicked come to power, people conceal themselves.
Whoever conceals their sins will not prosper, but the one who confesses and renounces them finds mercy.
Blessed is the one who always fears God, but whoever hardens their heart falls into disaster.
A wicked ruler with power over a poor people is like a roaring lion or a charging bear.
A tyrannical ruler lacks understanding, but one who hates unjust gain will prolong their days.
Anyone burdened with the guilt of murder will be a fugitive until death; let no one support them.
The one who walks blamelessly will be kept safe, but the one whose ways are crooked will fall suddenly.
Those who work their land will have plenty of food, but those who pursue fantasies will have plenty of poverty.
A faithful person will abound with blessings, but one eager to get rich will not go unpunished.
To show partiality is not good, yet for a piece of bread a person will do wrong.
The stingy hurry after wealth and do not realise that poverty awaits them.
Whoever rebukes a person will find more favour in the end than one who flatters with the tongue.
Whoever robs their father or mother and says, “It is not wrong,” is a companion to one who destroys.
The arrogant stir up strife, but those who trust in the Lord will prosper.
Those who trust in themselves are fools, but those who walk wisely will be kept safe.
Those who give to the poor will lack nothing, but those who shut their eyes to them receive many curses.
When the wicked come to power, people conceal themselves; but when they are gone, the righteous flourish.
A person who frequently receives correction yet remains obstinate will suddenly face consequences beyond remedy.
When the righteous flourish, the people rejoice; but when the wicked govern, the people lament.
A man who cherishes wisdom brings happiness to his father, but one who associates with harlots squanders his wealth.
A king gives stability to the land through justice, but those who accept bribes undermine it.
Those who flatter their neighbour are laying a snare for their steps.
Wrongdoers are trapped by their own sin, but the righteous sing and rejoice.
The righteous care about the rights of the poor; the wicked show no such concern.
Mockers incite a city, but the wise turn away wrath.
When a wise person engages in a dispute with a fool, the fool either rages or mocks, and there is no peace.
Bloodthirsty individuals hate the person of integrity, but the upright seek to protect them.
Fools give full vent to their anger, but the wise hold it back.
If a ruler listens to falsehood, all his officials become corrupt.
The poor and the tyrant share this: the Lord lights up the eyes of both.
When a king judges the poor with fairness, his reign will be firmly established.
The rod and reproof impart wisdom, but a child left to his own devices brings shame to his mother.
When the wicked multiply, transgression increases; but the righteous will witness their downfall.
Discipline your child, and they will give you rest; they will bring delight to your soul.
Where there is no vision, the people cast off restraint; but blessed is the one who follows the law.
A servant cannot be corrected by words alone; for though they understand, they will not respond.
Do you see someone who is hasty in their speech? There is more hope for a fool than for them.
One who indulges a servant from youth will find him rebellious in the end.
An angry person stirs up strife, and a hot- tempered person abounds in sin.
Pride brings a person low, but a humble spirit gains honour.
An accomplice of a thief is their own enemy; they hear the curse but reveal nothing.
The fear of others lays a snare, but whoever trusts in the Lord is kept safe.
Many seek the favour of a ruler, but justice for humanity comes from the Lord.
The righteous detest the unjust; the wicked detest the upright.
The sayings of Agur, son of Jakeh, the pronouncement. This man declares to Ithiel, to Ithiel and Ucal: “I am weary, God, but I can prevail.
Surely I am more ignorant than any other, and I lack human understanding.
I have not acquired wisdom, nor do I possess the knowledge of the Holy One.

Who has ascended to heaven and descended? Who has gathered the wind in his hands? Who has wrapped the waters in a garment? Who has established all the ends of the earth? What is his name, and what is his son’s name? Surely you know!
Every word of God is flawless; he is a shield to those who seek refuge in him.
Do not add to his words, or he will rebuke you and you will be found a liar.
Two things I ask of you, Lord; do not deny me before I die:
Keep falsehood and deceit far from me; give me neither poverty nor wealth, but provide me with my allotted portion.
Otherwise, I may have too much and deny you and say, ‘Who is the Lord?’ Or I may become impoverished and steal, and so dishonour the name of my God.
Do not slander a servant to their master, or they will curse you, and you will be found guilty.
Some curse their fathers and fail to bless their mothers;
those who consider themselves pure but have not been cleansed from their wrongdoing;
those whose eyes are ever so haughty, whose glances are filled with arrogance;
those whose teeth are like swords and whose jaws are set with knives to devour the poor from the earth and the needy from among humanity.
The leech has two daughters, crying out, ‘Give! Give!’ There are three things that are never satisfied, four that never say, ‘Enough!’:
the grave, the barren womb, the earth that is never satisfied with water, and fire that never says, ‘Enough!’
The eye that mocks a father and scorns an aged mother will be pecked out by the ravens of the valley, and eaten by the vultures.
There are three things that are too wonderful for me, four that I do not understand:
the flight of an eagle in the sky, the movement of a snake on a rock, the course of a ship on the high seas, and the way of a man with a young woman.
Such is the behaviour of an unfaithful woman: she eats the food, and then cleans her mouth so she can state: ‘I have done nothing wrong.’
Under three things the earth trembles, under four it cannot endure:
a servant who becomes king, a fool who is satisfied with food,
an unloved woman who gets married, and a servant who displaces her mistress.
Four small creatures on earth are exceedingly wise:
Ants, though small and weak, store up their food in the summer;
Hyraxes, though not powerful, make their home in the crags;
Locusts, having no king, advance together in ranks;
A lizard, easily caught by hand, yet found in kings’ palaces.
There are three things that are stately in their stride, four that move with noble bearing:
A lion, majestic among creatures, who retreats from nothing;
A proud rooster, a he-goat, and a king secure against revolt.
If you have been foolish in exalting yourself, or if you have devised evil, put your hand over your mouth.
For as the churning of milk produces butter, and as twisting the nose produces blood, so stirring up anger produces strife.
The sayings of King Lemuel, an inspired message his mother imparted to him:
How shall I address you, my son? How shall I address you, child of my womb? How shall I address you, child of my vows?
Do not give your strength to women, nor your ways to those who ruin kings.
It is not for kings, Lemuel, it is not for kings to drink wine, nor for rulers to crave strong drink,
lest they drink and forget what has been decreed, and pervert the rights of all the oppressed.
Let strong drink be given to those who are perishing, and wine to those in bitter distress.
Let them drink and forget their poverty, and remember their trouble no more.
Advocate for those who cannot speak for themselves, for the rights of all who are destitute.
Speak up, judge righteously, and defend the rights of the poor and needy.
A wife of noble character, who can find her? Her worth is far above rubies.
Her husband has full confidence in her, and he will have no lack of gain.
She brings him good and not harm, throughout her life.
She seeks wool and flax, and works with eager hands.
She is like a fleet of merchant ships, bringing her food from afar.
She rises while it is still night, and provides food for her household, and portions for her maidservants.
She evaluates a field and purchases it; with her earnings she plants a vineyard.
She prepares herself with strength and fortifies her arms.
She perceives that her trading is profitable, and her lamp does not go out at night.
She lays her hands to the distaff, and her hands hold the spindle.
She reaches out to the poor and extends her hands to the needy.
She has no fear for her household when it snows, for all of them are clothed in scarlet.
She makes coverings for her bed; her clothing is fine linen and purple.
Her husband is respected at the city gates, where he takes his seat among the elders of the land.
She crafts linen garments and sells them, and provides the merchants with sashes.
Strength and dignity are her clothing, and she can laugh at the days to come.
Her words are filled with wisdom, and she teaches kindness: it is ever present on her tongue.
She oversees the activities of her household, and does not partake in idleness.
Her children stand and bless her; her husband also praises her:
“Many women do noble things, but you excel them all.”
Charm can be misleading, and beauty does not last; but a woman who reveres the Lord is to be praised.
Honour her for all that her hands have done, and let her works bring her praise at the city gates.
The sayings of the Teacher, descendant of David, ruler in Jerusalem.
“Utter futility,” says the Teacher, “Utter futility! Everything is meaningless.”
What do people gain from all their work at which they toil under the sun?
Generations come and go, yet the earth remains unchanged through time.
The sun rises and the sun sets, and hurries back to its place where it rises again.
The wind blows towards the south and turns to the north; round and round it goes, returning on its course.
All rivers flow into the sea, yet the sea is never full. To the place where the rivers come from, there they return again.
All things are wearisome, more than one can express. The eye is not satisfied with seeing, nor the ear filled with hearing.
What has been will be again, and what has been done will be done again; there is nothing new under the sun.
Is there anything of which one can say, “Look! This is something new”? It has already existed in ages past.
There is no remembrance of former things, nor will there be any remembrance of later things by those who come after.
I, the Teacher, have served as king over Israel in Jerusalem.
I applied my mind to seek and explore by wisdom all that is done under heaven. It is a burdensome task God has given to the sons of men.
I have seen all things done under the sun; all of them are meaningless and a mere chasing after the wind.
What is crooked cannot be made straight; what is lacking cannot be counted.
I said to myself, “Behold, I have increased in wisdom more than anyone who has ruled over Jerusalem before me; I have experienced a great deal of wisdom and knowledge.”
Then I applied my mind to know wisdom and also madness and folly, but I learned that this too is a chasing after the wind.
For with much wisdom comes much sorrow; the more knowledge, the more pain.
I said to myself, “Come now, I will test you with pleasure to discover what is worthwhile.” Yet this too turned out to be futile.
I said of laughter, “It is madness,” and of pleasure, “What does it achieve?”
I sought to cheer myself with wine, embracing folly—my mind still guiding me with wisdom—to discern what is good for people to do under the heavens during the brief span of their lives.
I embarked on great works: I constructed houses for myself and planted vineyards.
I created gardens and parks, and planted all kinds of fruit trees within them.
I constructed reservoirs to water groves of flourishing trees.
I acquired male and female servants, and had servants born in my household. I also owned more herds and flocks than anyone before me in Jerusalem.
I accumulated silver and gold for myself, and the treasures of kings and provinces. I procured male and female singers, and the pleasures of men: many concubines.
I became greater than anyone who had preceded me in Jerusalem. In all this, my wisdom remained with me.
I denied my eyes nothing they desired; I withheld no pleasure from my heart. My heart rejoiced in all my labour, and this was the reward for all my toil.
Yet when I surveyed all that my hands had accomplished and what I had toiled to achieve, everything was meaningless, a chasing after the wind; there was no profit under the sun.
Then I turned my thoughts to consider wisdom, madness, and folly. What more can the successor to the king do than what has already been done?
I saw that wisdom is superior to folly, just as light is superior to darkness.
The wise have eyes in their heads, while the fool walks in darkness; yet I realised that the same fate befalls them both.
Then I said to myself, “The fate of the fool will overtake me also. What then is the benefit of being wise?” I said to myself, “This too is meaningless.”
For the wise, like the fool, will not be remembered for long; in days to come both will be forgotten. Like the fool, the wise too must die!
So I loathed life, because the work done under the sun was grievous to me. All of it is meaningless, a chasing after the wind.
I detested all the things I had toiled for under the sun, because I must leave them to the one who comes after me.
And who can tell if that person will be wise or foolish? Yet they will have authority over all the results of my toil into which I have poured my effort and skill under the sun. This too is futile.
So my heart was filled with despair over all my labourious work under the sun.
For a person may labour with wisdom, knowledge, and skill, and then they must leave all they own to another who has not toiled for it. This too is futile and a great misfortune.
What do people gain from all their toil and anxious striving with which they labour under the sun?
All their days their work brings grief and pain; even at night their minds do not rest. This too is futile.
There is nothing better for a person than to eat, drink, and find satisfaction in their toil. This too, I see, is from the hand of God,
for who can eat or find enjoyment apart from Him?
To the person who pleases Him, God gives wisdom, knowledge, and happiness, but to the sinner, He assigns the task of gathering and storing up wealth to hand it over to the one who pleases God.
This too is meaningless, a chasing after the wind. There is a time appointed for everything, and a season for every activity under the heavens:
A time to be born and a time to pass away, a time to plant and a time to uproot what is planted,
A time to take life and a time to heal, a time to dismantle and a time to construct,
A time to shed tears and a time to laugh, a time to grieve and a time to dance,
A time to disperse stones and a time to collect them, a time to embrace and a time to hold back from embracing,
A time to search and a time to abandon the search, a time to keep and a time to discard,
A time to rip off and a time to repair, a time to be hold your tongue and a time when you must speak,
A time to love and a time to dislike, a time for conflict and a time for peace.
What do workers gain from their labour?
I have seen the task God has given to the human race to occupy themselves.
He has made everything beautiful in its time. He has also placed eternity in the human heart, yet no one can comprehend what God has done from beginning to end.
I know that there is nothing better for people than to rejoice and to do good in their lifetime.
Moreover, that each person may eat and drink and find satisfaction in all their toil—this is the gift of God.
I know that everything God does will endure forever; nothing can be added to it, and nothing taken from it. God does this so that people will fear Him.
Whatever is has already been, and what will be has been before; and God calls the past to account.
Furthermore, I have seen under the sun that in the place of judgment there is wickedness, and in the place of justice there is wickedness.
I said to myself, “God will bring into judgment both the righteous and the wicked, for there is a time for every matter and every deed.”
I also said to myself regarding humans, “God tests them so that they may see that they are like the animals.”
For the fate of human beings and the fate of animals is the same: As one dies, so dies the other. They all have the same breath; humans have no advantage over animals. Everything is meaningless.
All return to the same place; all come from dust, and to dust all return.
Who can determine if the human spirit ascends upward and if the spirit of the animal descends into the earth?
So I saw that there is nothing better than for a person to be happy in their work, because that is their lot.

For who can bring them to see what will happen after them? Once more, I observed all the acts of oppression that occur under the sun. I saw the tears of the oppressed, and they had no comforter; power was on the side of their oppressors, and they had no comforter.
I concluded that the dead who have already passed are more fortunate than the living who are still alive.
Yet better than both is the one who has never been born, who has not witnessed the evil deeds done under the sun.
I noticed that all labour and all achievement stem from one person's rivalry with another. This too is vanity, a pursuit of the wind.
The fool sits idly and brings ruin upon himself.
Better is one handful with tranquillity than two handfuls with toil and chasing after the wind.
Again, I observed something futile under the sun:

There was a man who was alone, without a son or brother. There was no end to his toil, yet his eyes were not satisfied with wealth. "For whom am I toiling," he asked, "and depriving myself of enjoyment?" This too is vanity, and a miserable task.
Two are more effective than one, as they have a good return for their efforts:
If one falls, the other can lift him up. But pity anyone who falls and has no one to help them up.
Moreover, if two lie down together, they will keep warm. But how can one keep warm alone?
Though one may be overpowered, two can defend themselves. A cord of three strands is not easily broken.
Better is a poor but wise youth than an old and foolish king who no longer knows how to take advice.
For he may have come from prison to become king, even though he was born poor in his kingdom.
I observed all who live and walk under the sun following the youth, the successor to the throne.
There is no end to all the people who were before them, yet those who come later will not be pleased with him.

This too is vanity, a chasing after the wind. Be cautious as you enter the house of God, and approach to listen rather than to present the offering of fools, who are unaware that they are doing wrong.
Do not be rash with your mouth, nor hasty in your heart to bring up a matter before God. For God is in heaven and you are on earth, so let your words be few.
For dreams come with much busyness, and a fool's voice with many words.
When you make a vow to God, do not delay in fulfilling it. He takes no pleasure in fools; fulfil what you vow.
It is better not to vow than to make a vow and not fulfil it.
Do not let your mouth lead you into sin, and do not say before the messenger of God that it was a mistake. Why should God be angry at your words and destroy the work of your hands?
For in many dreams and many words, there is futility. Instead, stand in awe of God.

If you see the oppression of the poor and the denial of justice and rights in a province, do not be astonished at the matter; for one official is watched by a higher one, and there are higher officials over them.
The produce of the land is shared by all; even the king benefits from the fields.
Whoever loves money will never have enough; whoever loves wealth will not be satisfied with their income. This too is vanity.
As goods increase, so do those who consume them. What advantage then do the owners have except to gaze upon them?
The sleep of the labourer is sweet, whether he eats little or much, but the abundance of the rich will not allow them to sleep.
There is a grievous evil I have seen under the sun: wealth hoarded to the detriment of its owner,
or wealth lost through unfortunate circumstances, leaving nothing for their children to inherit.
As one comes naked from their mother's womb, so they will depart as they came. They will take nothing from their toil that they can carry in their hands.
This too is a grievous evil: exactly as one comes, so they will depart. What gain is there for those who toil for the wind?
All their days they eat in darkness, with great frustration, sickness, and anger.

This is what I have observed to be good and fitting: to eat, to drink, and to find satisfaction in all one's labour under the sun during the few days of life God has given them—for this is their reward.
Moreover, when God gives someone wealth and possessions, and the ability to enjoy them, to accept their lot and find joy in their toil—this is a gift from God.
They seldom reflect on the days of their life, because God keeps them occupied with joy in their heart.
I have observed an evil under the sun, and it is prevalent among humanity:

God grants individuals riches, wealth, and honour, so that they lack nothing their heart desires, yet God does not allow them the capacity to enjoy them, and instead, a stranger enjoys them. This is vanity and a severe misfortune.
If a man has a hundred children and lives many years, no matter how long, but he cannot enjoy his wealth and does not receive a proper burial, then I say a stillborn child is better off than he.
For it comes in futility and departs into darkness, and its name is enveloped in darkness.
Though it has not seen the sun or known anything, it finds more rest than that man.
Even if he lives two thousand years but does not enjoy his prosperity—do not all go to the same place?
All one's labour is for the mouth, yet the appetite is never satisfied.
What advantage does the wise have over the fool? What benefit does the poor have in knowing how to conduct themselves among the living?
Better is what the eyes see than the wandering of desire. This too is vanity, a pursuit of the wind.
Whatever exists has already been named, and what humanity is has been known; no one can contend with one who is mightier.
The more words, the greater the vanity, and what profit is there to anyone?
For who knows what is good for a person in life, during the few and futile days they pass like a shadow?
Who can inform them what will occur under the sun after they are gone? A good name is more desirable than fine oil, and the day of death is better than the day of birth.
It is preferable to visit a house of mourning than a house of feasting, for death is the fate of everyone, and the living should take this to heart.
Sorrow is preferable to laughter, for a sad face may lead to a happy heart.
The heart of the wise is in the house of mourning, while the heart of fools is in the house of pleasure.
It is better to heed the rebuke of the wise than to listen to the song of fools.
As the crackling of thorns under a pot, so is the laughter of fools. This too is vanity.
Oppression may make a wise person act foolishly, and a bribe corrupts the heart.
The conclusion of a matter is better than its beginning, and patience is superior to pride.
Do not be quick to anger, for anger resides in the bosom of fools.
Do not ask, “Why were the former days better than these?” For it is not wise to ask such questions.
Wisdom, like an inheritance, is a good thing and a benefit to those who see the sun.
Wisdom is a shelter, as money is a shelter, but the advantage of knowledge is that wisdom preserves the life of its possessor.
Consider the work of God: Who can straighten what He has made crooked?
In times of prosperity be joyful, but in times of adversity consider: God has made one as well as the other, so that no one can discover what will come after them.
In my fleeting life, I have seen both: the righteous perishing in their righteousness, and the wicked living long in their wickedness.
Do not be excessively righteous, nor be overly wise— why should you bring trouble upon yourself?
Do not be overly wicked, and do not be foolish— why should you die before your time?
It is good to grasp one and not let go of the other. Whoever fears God will avoid all extremes.
Wisdom gives strength to a wise person more than ten rulers in a city.
Truly, there is no one on earth who is perfectly righteous, no one who always does what is right and never sins.
Do not pay close attention to every word people say, or you might hear your servant speaking ill of you—
for you know in your heart that you have also spoken ill of others many times.
I applied all this to wisdom and said, “I will be wise”— but it was beyond me.
What exists is distant and deep— who can discover it?
So I turned my mind to understand, to explore and to search out wisdom and the scheme of things, and to understand the folly of wickedness and the madness of folly.
I find more bitter than death the woman who ensnares, whose heart is a trap and whose hands are chains. The man who pleases God will escape her, but the sinner she will capture.
“Behold,” says the Teacher, “this is what I have discovered: Adding one thing to another to find an explanation—
while I was still searching but not finding— I found one upright man among a thousand, but not a single upright woman among them all.
This only have I found: God created mankind upright, but they have sought out many schemes.”
Who is comparable to the wise, and who understands the meaning of a matter? Wisdom brightens a person's face and softens its sternness.
I advise you to keep the king's command because of the oath made before God.
Do not be quick to leave his presence. Do not support an evil matter, for he will act as he wishes.
Since the king's word is authoritative, who can question him, “What are you doing?”
Whoever obeys his command will face no harm, and a wise heart knows the proper time and procedure.
For there is a proper time and procedure for every matter, though a person's troubles weigh heavily upon them.
